The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings.
Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats.
A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.

The National Academy of Sports Medicine (NASM) Code of Professional Conduct is designed to assist both certified and non-certified members in upholding the highest levels of professional and ethical conduct.
Each certified or non-certified NASM member must provide optimal professional service and demonstrate excellent client care.
Each NASM member must abide fully by the NASM Code of Professional Conduct.
Each NASM member must conduct themselves in a manner that merits the respect of the public, other colleagues, and NASM.
Each NASM member must treat each colleague and client with the utmost respect and dignity.
NASM members must not make false or derogatory assumptions concerning the practices of colleagues and clients.
NASM members must use appropriate professional communication in all verbal, nonverbal, and written transactions.
Each NASM member must provide and maintain an environment that ensures client safety.
NASM members shall not diagnose or treat illness or injury (except for basic first aid) unless they are legally licensed to do so and are working in that capacity at that time.

NASM members shall not train clients with a diagnosed health condition unless they have been specifically trained to do so, are following procedures prescribed and supervised by a valid licensed medical professional, or are legally licensed to do so and are working in that capacity at that time.
NASM members shall not begin to train a client before receiving and reviewing a current health history questionnaire signed by the client.
NASM members must hold a CPR and AED certification at all times.
NASM members must refer the client to the appropriate medical practitioner when they become aware of any change in the client’s health status or medication.
NASM members must refer the client to the appropriate medical practitioner when they become aware of an undiagnosed illness, injury, or risk factor.

NASM members must refer the client to the appropriate medical practitioner when they become aware of any unusual client pain or discomfort during the course of the training session that warrants professional care after the session has been discontinued and assessed.

NASM members must refer the client to other healthcare professionals when nutritional and supplemental advice is requested unless they have been specifically trained to do so or hold a credential to do so and are acting in that capacity at the time.
NASM members must maintain a level of personal hygiene appropriate for a health and fitness setting.
NASM members must wear clothing that is clean, modest, and professional.
NASM members must remain in good standing and maintain current certification status by acquiring all necessary continuing-education requirements.
Each certified and non-certified NASM member shall respect the confidentiality of all client information.

NASM members must protect the client’s confidentiality in conversations, advertisements, and any other arena, unless otherwise agreed to by the client in writing, or as a result of medical or legal necessity.
NASM members must protect the interest of clients who are minors by law, or who are unable to give voluntary consent by securing the legal permission of the appropriate third party or guardian.
NASM members must store and dispose of client records in a secure manner.
Each certified or non-certified NASM member must comply with all legal requirements within the applicable jurisdiction.
NASM members must obey all local, state, provincial, or federal laws.
NASM members must accept complete responsibility for their actions.
NASM members must maintain accurate and truthful records.
NASM members must respect and uphold all existing publishing and copyright laws.
Each certified or non-certified NASM member must practice with honesty, integrity, and lawfulness.
NASM members must maintain adequate liability insurance.
NASM members must maintain adequate and truthful progress notes for each client.
NASM members must accurately and truthfully inform the public of services rendered.
NASM members must honestly and truthfully represent all professional qualifications and affiliations.
NASM members must advertise in a manner that is honest, dignified, and representative of services that can be delivered without the use of provocative or sexual language or pictures.
NASM members must maintain accurate financial, contract, appointment, and tax records including original receipts for a minimum of four years.
NASM members must comply with all local, state, federal, or providence laws regarding sexual harassment.
NASM has been the leading authority in certification, continuing education, and career development for health and fitness professionals for more than 25 years.
NASM is the world’s foremost resource for fitness and sports medicine information.
The Optimum Performance Training™ (OPT™) model is the industry’s first evidence-based training system founded on the scientific rationale of human movement science.
It is imperative that health and fitness professionals fully understand all components of exercise programming.

NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training, Sixth Edition, helps you to master goal-specific program design, accurate assessment, and development and modification of exercise in a safe and effective manner.
Objectives open each chapter and present learning goals to help you focus on and retain the crucial topics discussed.

Chimera et al. (2004) in a pretest and posttest with control group design using 20 healthy Division I female athletes found that a 6-week plyometric training program improved hip abductor and adductor coactivation ratios to help control varus (bowlegged) and valgus (knock-knees) moments at the knee during landing.

Wilkerson et al. (2004) in a quasi-experimental design with 19 female basketball players demonstrated that a 6-week plyometric training program improved hamstring to quadriceps ratio, which has been shown to enhance dynamic knee stability during the eccentric deceleration phase of landing.
Luebbers et al. (2003) in a randomized controlled trial with 19 subjects demonstrated that 4-and 7-week plyometric training programs enhanced anaerobic power and vertical jump height.

Hewett et al. (1996) in a prospective study demonstrated decreased peak landing forces, enhanced muscle-balance ratio between the quadriceps and hamstrings, and decreased rate of anterior cruciate ligament injuries in female soccer, basketball, and volleyball players who incorporated plyometric training into their program.
