A large number of clients seeking fitness and exercise training advice from personal trainers will currently be under the care of a physician or another medical professional and may be taking one or more prescribed medications.

It is not the role of the personal trainer to administer, prescribe, or educate clients on the usage and effects of any form of legally prescribed medication by a licensed physician or other health-care provider.
Personal trainers should always consult with their client’s physician or medical professionals regarding the client’s health information and which if any medications they may be currently taking.
Beta-blockers are generally used as antihypertensive (high blood pressure), may also be prescribed for arrhythmias (irregular heart rate).
Calcium-channel blockers are generally prescribed for hypertension and angina (chest pain).
Nitrates are generally prescribed for hypertension, congestive heart failure.
Diuretics are generally prescribed for hypertension, congestive heart failure, and peripheral edema.
Bronchodilators are generally prescribed to correct or prevent bronchial smooth muscle constriction in individuals with asthma and other pulmonary diseases.
Vasodilators are used in the treatment of hypertension and congestive heart failure.
Antidepressants are used in the treatment of various psychiatric and emotional disorders.

Personal trainers are able to gain insight into their clients’ daily physical activity level and health by gathering subjective information about their personal history, including occupation, lifestyle, and medical background.

The Physical Activity Readiness Questionnaire (PAR-Q) identifies clients who are at high risk for developing cardiovascular disease, and thus require a medical referral before starting an exercise program.

By asking questions that provide important information about the structure and function of a client, it is possible to assess risk factors for chronic disease as well as movement capacities and what kinds of movement patterns are performed throughout the day.
Questions about recreational activities and hobbies reflect what clients do in their leisure time.
Proper forms of training for specific activities must be incorporated to increase the efficiency of the human movement system, while avoiding injury.
Clients with sedentary hobbies will probably not be at the same level of training as those who participate in recreational sports.
Finding out a client’s medical history is crucial to ensuring a safe, effective, and enjoyable exercise experience.

Past injuries affect the functioning of the human movement system and are important to ask clients about, as well as surgical procedures that may have similar effects as injuries because they cause pain and inflammation, which can alter neural control to the affected muscles and joints if not rehabilitated properly.
It is also important to ask clients about any other chronic health conditions, which are likely to occur in individuals who are habitually sedentary.
Personal trainers should consult with a client’s physician or other medical professionals if they have any questions regarding medications used by one of their clients.

Objective information collected during a fitness assessment includes resting and exercise physiological measurements (e.g., blood pressure, heart rate), resting anthropometric measurements (e.g., height, weight, body fat percentage, circumference measurements), and specific measures of fitness (e.g., muscular endurance, flexibility, cardiorespiratory fitness).
Objective information collected during a fitness assessment can be used to compare beginning baseline measures of fitness with measurements taken weeks, months, or even years later.

When follow-up data are compared with baseline measurements, ideally improvements of all categories of health-related physical fitness are evident, confirming the effectiveness of the training program.

Categories of objective information include: Physiological measurements, Body composition assessments, Cardiorespiratory assessments, Static posture assessment, Movement assessments (dynamic posture), Performance assessments.
The assessment of resting heart rate (HR) and blood pressure (BP) is a sensitive indicator of a client’s overall cardiorespiratory health as well as fitness status.

Through the initial assessment and reassessment of a client’s HR and BP, personal trainers are able to gather valuable information that helps in the design, monitoring, and progression of a client’s exercise program.

Resting HR is a fairly good indicator of overall cardiorespiratory fitness, whereas exercise HR is a strong indicator of how a client’s cardiorespiratory system is responding and adapting to exercise.
A pulse is created by blood moving or pulsating through arteries each time the heart contracts.
Each time the heart contracts or beats, one wave of blood flow or pulsation of blood can be felt by placing one or two fingers on an artery.
The artery contracts and relaxes periodically to rhythmically force the blood along its way circulating throughout the body.
This coincides with the contraction and relaxation of the heart as it pumps the blood through the arteries and veins.
Therefore the pulse rate is also known as the heart rate.

There are seven pulse points, or places where arteries come close enough to the skin to be able to have a pulse felt; the two most common sites used to record a pulse are the radial and carotid arteries.
Heart rate can be recorded on the inside of the wrist (radial pulse; preferred) or on the neck to the side of the windpipe (carotid pulse; use with caution).
To gather an accurate recording, it is best to teach clients how to record their resting HR on rising in the morning.
Instruct them to test their resting heart rate three mornings in a row and average the three readings.
To find the radial pulse, lightly place two fingers along the right side of the arm in line and just above the thumb.
Once a pulse is felt, count the pulses for 60 seconds.
Record the 60-second pulse rate and average over the course of 3 days.
To find the carotid pulse, lightly place two fingers on the neck, just to the side of the larynx.
Once a pulse is identified, count the pulses for 60 seconds.
Record the 60-second pulse rate and average over the course of 3 days.
Resting HR can vary.
As discussed in chapter three, the typical resting heart rate is between 70 and 80 beats per minute.
However, on average, the resting HR for a male is 70 beats per minute and 75 beats per minute for a female.
Personal trainers can use a client’s resting HR to calculate the target heart rate (THR) zones in which a client should perform cardiorespiratory exercise.
The two most common ways to calculate THR are to use a percentage of the client’s maximum heart rate (MHR) or to use a formula based on age and gender.
A client’s estimated maximal heart rate (HR max) is found by subtracting their age from 220 (220 - age).

Once the client’s HR max is determined, multiply the estimated HR max by the appropriate intensity (65–95%) at which the client should work while performing cardiorespiratory exercise to calculate THR.
Zone one: Maximal heart rate x 0.65
Zone two: Maximal heart rate x 0.75
Zone three: Maximal heart rate x 0.76
Zone four: Maximal heart rate x 0.85
Zone five: Maximal heart rate x 0.86
Zone six: Maximal heart rate x 0.95
Exercise intensity levels may need to be lower than 65% (e.g., ~40 to 55%) depending on the client’s initial physical condition status.

Heart rate reserve (HRR), also known as the Karvonen method, is a method of establishing training intensity on the basis of the difference between a client’s predicted maximal heart rate and their resting heart rate.
THR = [(HR max - HR rest) x desired intensity] + HR rest
Blood pressure (BP) is the pressure of the circulating blood against the walls of the blood vessels after blood is ejected from the heart.
Systolic blood pressure is the pressure within the arterial system after the heart contracts.
Diastolic blood pressure is the pressure within the arterial system when the heart is resting and filling with blood.
An example of a blood pressure reading is 120/80 (120 over 80).
In this example, 120 is the systolic number and 80 is the diastolic number.
Blood pressure measurements always consist of both readings.
An acceptable systolic blood pressure measurement for health is 120 millimeters (mm) of mercury (Hg) or mm Hg.
An acceptable diastolic blood pressure is 80 mm Hg.
Blood pressure is measured using an aneroid sphygmomanometer, which consists of an inflatable cuff, a pressure dial, a bulb with a valve, and a stethoscope.
To determine systolic pressure, listen for the first observation of the pulse.
Diastolic pressure is determined when the pulse fades away.
It is highly recommended that anyone, including personal trainers, take a professional course in blood pressure assessment before assessing blood pressure with any clients.
Body composition refers to the relative percentage of body weight that is fat versus fat-free tissue, or more commonly reported as ‘Percent Body Fat.’

Fat-free mass can be defined as body weight except stored fat, and includes muscles, bones, water, connective and organ tissues, and teeth, whereas fat mass includes both essential fat (crucial for normal body functioning) and nonessential fat (storage fat or adipose tissue).

Benefits of body composition assessments include: identifying client’s health risk for excessively high or low levels of body fat, promoting client’s understanding of body fat, monitoring changes in body composition, helping to estimate healthy body weight for clients and athletes, assisting in exercise program design, using as a motivational tool (for certain clients), monitoring changes in body composition that are associated with chronic diseases, and assessing effectiveness of nutrition and exercise choices.
Currently, there is no accepted percent of body fat standard for all ages because most body composition studies use small groups, usually young adults.
These studies demonstrate that body fat typically ranges from 10 to 20% for men and 20 to 30% for women (53).
Based on these studies, recommendations of 15% for men and 25% for women have been made, discussed in more detail in Table 6.5 and Table 6.6 (53).
There are a variety of methods used to estimate body composition; they vary according to cost, accuracy, and skill needed to perform them.
Skinfold measurement uses a caliper to estimate the amount of subcutaneous fat beneath the skin.

Bioelectrical impedance uses a portable instrument to conduct an electrical current through the body to estimate fat. This form of assessment is based on the hypothesis that tissues that are high in water content conduct electrical currents with less resistance than those with little water (such as adipose tissue).
Underwater weighing, often referred to as hydrostatic weighing, has been the most common technique used in exercise physiology laboratories to determine body composition.
The fact that bone, muscle, and connective tissue, collectively known as lean mass, sinks, whereas body fat floats is the main principle behind hydrostatic testing.
In essence, a person’s weight is compared with a person’s weight underwater to determine fat percentage.

Because bone and muscle are denser than water, a person with a larger percentage of lean body mass will weigh more in the water and ultimately have a lower body fat percentage versus someone with less lean body mass.
A person with more body fat will have a lighter body in water and a higher percentage of body fat.
Most personal trainers do not have an exercise physiology laboratory at their disposal, so the skinfold caliper method will be the method emphasized in this text.
Skinfolds (SKF) are an indirect measure of the thickness of subcutaneous adipose tissue.
The assumption is that the amount of fat present in the subcutaneous regions of the body is proportional to overall body fatness, and most of the time this is the case.

Recommendations for assessing body composition using skinfolds include: training with an individual skilled in SKF assessment and frequently comparing your results with theirs, taking a minimum of two measurements at each site, and each site must be within 1 to 2 mm to take an average at each site, opening the jaw of the caliper before removing it from the site, being meticulous when locating anatomic landmarks, not measuring SKFs immediately after exercise, instructing the clients ahead of time regarding the test protocol, and avoiding performing SKFs on extremely obese clients.
The National Academy of Sports Medicine uses the Durnin formula (sometimes known as the Durnin–Womersley formula) to calculate a client’s percentage of body fat.
This formula was chosen for its simple four-site upper body measurement process.
The Durnin formula’s four sites of skinfold measurement are the biceps, triceps, subscapular, and iliac crest.
All skinfold measurements should be taken on the right side of the body.

Aft  er the four sites have been measured, add the totals of the four sites, find the appropriate sex and age categories for the body composition on the Durnin–Womersley body fat percentage calculation table.
For example, a 40-year-old female client with the sum of the skinfolds of 40 has a percent body fat of 28.14% (or round down to 28%).
Assessing body fat using skinfold calipers can be a sensitive situation, particularly for very overweight individuals.
The accuracy of the skinfold measurement in these situations typically decreases; thus, it would be more appropriate to not use this method for assessing body fat.
Assessing body composition can determine the proportion of an individual's body weight that comes from fat and lean body mass.
Body fat % / Scale weight / Fat mass.
Scale weight / Fat mass / Lean body mass.
If a 40-year-old female client weighs 130 pounds, her fat mass would be 36 pounds and her lean body mass would be 94 pounds.
Circumference measurements are affected by both fat and muscle and do not provide accurate estimates of fatness in the general population.
Circumference measurements can be used on obese clients.
Circumference measurements are good for comparisons and progressions.
Circumference measurements are good for assessing fat pattern and distribution.
Circumference measurements are inexpensive.
Circumference measurements are easy to record.
Circumference measurements have little technician error.
Circumference measurements are used for waist circumference.
Circumference measurements are used for waist-to-hip ratio (WHR).
Circumference measurements are designed to assess girth changes in the body.
Consistency is the most important factor to consider when taking circumference measurements.
Neck circumference is measured across the Adam’s apple.
Chest circumference is measured across the nipple line.
Waist circumference is measured at the narrowest point of the waist, below the rib cage and just above the top of the hipbones.
Hips circumference is measured with feet together at the widest portion of the buttocks.
Thigh circumference is measured 10 inches above the top of the patella.
Calves circumference is measured at the maximal circumference between the ankle and the knee.
Biceps circumference is measured at the maximal circumference of the biceps with the arm extended, palm facing forward.
The waist-to-hip ratio is computed by dividing the waist measurement by the hip measurement.
A waist-to-hip ratio greater than 0.80 for women and greater than 0.95 for men may put individuals at risk for a number of diseases.
Body mass index (BMI) is a rough assessment based on the concept that a person’s weight should be proportional to their height.
An elevated BMI is linked to an increased risk of disease, especially if associated with a large waist circumference.
BMI is calculated by dividing the weight in kilograms by the square of the height in meters or dividing body weight in pounds by the square of height in inches and multiplying by 703.
The lowest risk for disease lies within a BMI range of 22 to 24.9.
Scientific evidence indicates that the risk for disease increases with a BMI of 25 or greater.
Individuals who are underweight are also at risk.
Objective information such as heart rate, blood pressure, and body composition provides measurable data that track changes in a client.
It can be motivating for a client to assess and reassess body fat, circumference measurements, hip-to-waist ratio, or body mass index.
Many clients want to lose body fat, and, as such, it is important to be able to determine a starting body fat percentage.
Skinfold calipers are one of the easiest ways to determine a starting body fat percentage in a gym setting.
Consistency in location and administration is vital when measuring skinfolds.

Calculate a client’s percentage of body fat by measuring four sites with the calipers, adding the totals of all four sites, and then finding the appropriate sex and age categories on the Durnin–Womersley body fat percentage calculation table.
Circumference measurements and waist-to-hip ratio are other sources of feedback that assess girth changes in the body.
A waist-to-hip ratio greater than 0.80 for women and greater than 0.95 for men may put these individuals at risk for a number of diseases.
The BMI is a good way to determine whether a client’s weight is appropriate for his or her height.
The chance of having obesity-related health problems potentially increases when a person’s BMI exceeds 25.
The most valid measurement for functional capacity of the cardiopulmonary (heart and lungs) system is Cardiopulmonary Exercise Testing (CPET), also known as maximal oxygen uptake (V. o  2max ).
CPET is not always practical to measure V. o  2max because of equipment requirements, time involved, and willingness of clients to perform at maximal physical capacity.
Submaximal tests are often the preferred method for determining cardiorespiratory functional capacity and fitness.
Submaximal testing allows for the prediction or estimation of V. o  2max.
These tests are similar to V. o  2max tests, but they differ in that they are terminated at a predetermined heart rate intensity or time frame.

There are multiple submaximal tests that have been shown to be valid and reliable predictors of V. o  2max, and these tests are often categorized by type (run/walk tests, cycle ergometer tests, and step tests).
Any of these tests can be used; however, the space or equipment constraints and specific population (e.g., elderly, youth) to be tested should be considered.
Two common submaximal tests for assessing cardiorespiratory efficiency are the YMCA 3-minute step test and the Rockport Walk Test.
The YMCA 3-minute step test is designed to estimate an individual’s cardiorespiratory fitness level on the basis of a submaximal bout of stair climbing at a set pace for 3 minutes.
Perform a 3-minute step test by having a client perform 96 steps per minute on a 12-inch step for a total of 3 minutes.
It is important that the client performs the step test with the correct cadence.
Within 5 seconds of completing the exercise, the client’s resting heart rate is measured for a period of 60 seconds and recorded as the recovery pulse.
Women aged 18–25 have an average fitness score of 104–110.
Women aged 26–35 have an average fitness score of 104–110.
Women aged 36–45 have an average fitness score of 107–112.
Women aged 46–55 have an average fitness score of 113–118.
Women aged 56–65 have an average fitness score of 113–118.
Women with a fitness score below average in the 18–25 age group have a score of 113–120.
Women with a fitness score below average in the 26–35 age group have a score of 113–119.
Women with a fitness score below average in the 36–45 age group have a score of 115–120.
Women with a fitness score below average in the 46–55 age group have a score of 120–124.
Women with a fitness score below average in the 56–65 age group have a score of 119–127.
Women with a fitness score of poor in the 18–25 age group have a score of 122–131.
Women with a fitness score of poor in the 26–35 age group have a score of 122–129.
Women with a fitness score of poor in the 36–45 age group have a score of 124–132.
Women with a fitness score of poor in the 46–55 age group have a score of 126–132.
Women with a fitness score of poor in the 56–65 age group have a score of 129–135.
Women with a fitness score of very poor in the 18–25 age group have a score of 135–169.
Women with a fitness score of very poor in the 26–35 age group have a score of 134–171.
Women with a fitness score of very poor in the 36–45 age group have a score of 137–169.
Women with a fitness score of very poor in the 46–55 age group have a score of 137–171.
Women with a fitness score of very poor in the 56–65 age group have a score of 141–174.
Women with a fitness score of very poor in the 56–65 age group have a score of 135–155.
The Rockport Walk Test is designed to estimate a cardiovascular starting point.
The starting point of the Rockport Walk Test is modified based on ability level.
To determine the oxygen consumption (V.o2) score, use the following formula: 132.853 / (0.0769 * Weight) / (0.3877 * Age) / (6.315 * Gender) / (3.2649 * Time) / (0.1565 * Heart rate) = V.o2 score.
Weight is measured in pounds (lbs).
Gender is represented as 1 for male and 0 for female.
Time is expressed in minutes and hundredths of minutes.
Heart rate is measured in beats per minute.
Age is measured in years.
Zone one is defined as 0.65 to 0.75 of maximal heart rate.
Zone two is defined as 0.76 to 0.85 of maximal heart rate.
Zone three is defined as 0.86 to 0.95 of maximal heart rate.
Two popular cardiorespiratory assessments that can be used to determine one’s starting point include the 3-minute step test and the Rockport Walk Test.
Neuromuscular efficiency is the ability of the nervous system and musculature system to communicate properly producing optimal movement.
Proper postural alignment allows optimal neuromuscular efficiency, which helps produce effective and safe movement.
Proper posture ensures that the muscles of the body are optimally aligned at the proper length-tension relationships necessary for efficient functioning of force-couples.
This allows for proper arthrokinematics (joint motion) and effective absorption and distribution of forces throughout the human movement system (kinetic chain), alleviating excess stress on joints.
Proper posture will help keep muscles at their proper length, allowing muscles to properly work together, ensuring proper joint motion, maximizing force production, and reducing the risk of injury.
Proper posture helps the body produce high levels of functional strength.
Without proper posture, the body may degenerate or experience poor posture, altered movement patterns, and muscle imbalances.
These dysfunctions can lead to common injuries such as ankle sprains, tendonitis, and low-back pain.
Static posture, or how an individual physically presents himself or herself in stance, could be considered the base from which an individual moves.
It is reflected in the alignment of the body.
It provides the foundation or the platform from which the extremities function.
As with any structure, a weak foundation leads to secondary problems elsewhere in the system.
The use of a static postural assessment has been the basis for identifying muscle imbalances.

A static postural assessment may not be able to specifically identify whether a problem is structural in nature or whether it is derived from the development of poor muscular recruitment patterns with resultant muscle imbalances.
However, a static postural assessment provides excellent indicators of problem areas that must be further evaluated to clarify the problems at hand.
This provides a basis for developing an exercise strategy to target causative factors of faulty movement and neuromuscular inefficiency.
How an individual presents himself or herself in static stance is in a sense, a road map of how he or she has been using his or her body with time.
What is interesting is that the body has a tendency to compensate in particular patterns or by particular relationships among muscles.
Janda identified three basic compensatory patterns.

It is not to say that other compensations do not occur. He simply suggested that there was a cascading effect of alterations or deviations in static posture that would more likely than not present themselves in a particular pattern.
The three postural distortion patterns to be assessed during a static postural assessment include the pronation distortion syndrome, the lower crossed syndrome, and the upper crossed syndrome.
Pronation distortion syndrome is a postural distortion syndrome characterized by foot pronation (flat feet) and adducted and internally rotated knees (knock knees).
Lower crossed syndrome is a postural distortion syndrome characterized by an anterior tilt to the pelvis (arched lower back).
Upper crossed syndrome is a postural distortion syndrome characterized by a forward head and rounded shoulders.
Upper crossed syndrome involves shortened gastrocnemius, soleus, hip flexor complex, and adductors muscles.
Upper crossed syndrome involves lengthened hamstring complex, posterior tibialis, gluteus maximus, gluteus medius, latissimus dorsi, and transversus abdominis muscles.
Upper crossed syndrome can lead to increased risk of hamstring complex strain, anterior knee pain, and low-back pain.
Lower crossed syndrome involves shortened upper trapezius, levator scapulae, serratus anterior, sternocleidomastoid, and scalenes muscles.

Lower crossed syndrome involves lengthened deep cervical flexors, rhomboids, rotator cuff, mid-trapezius, lower trapezius, teres major, teres minor, subscapularis, infraspinatus, pectoralis major/minor muscles.

Lower crossed syndrome can lead to increased risk of headaches, cervical extension, biceps tendonitis, scapular protraction/elevation, rotator cuff impingement, thoracic outlet syndrome, and shoulder extension.
A static postural assessment should check for neutral alignment, symmetry, balanced muscle tone, and specific postural deformities.
The client should be viewed in a weight-bearing position (standing) from multiple vantage points (anterior, posterior, lateral).
The fitness professional should look for gross deviations in overall posture.
Postural assessments require observation of the kinetic chain (human movement system).
NASM has devised the use of kinetic chain checkpoints to allow the personal trainer to systematically view the body in an organized fashion.
The kinetic chain checkpoints refer to major joint regions of the body including the foot and ankle, knee, lumbo-pelvic-hip complex (LPHC), shoulders, and head and cervical spine.
In the anterior view, the foot/ankles should be straight and parallel, not flattened or externally rotated.
In the anterior view, the knees should be in line with toes, not adducted or abducted.
In the anterior view, the LPHC should have the pelvis level with both anterior superior iliac spines in the same transverse plane.
In the anterior view, the shoulders should be level, not elevated or rounded.
In the anterior view, the head should be in a neutral position, not tilted nor rotated.
In the lateral view, the foot/ankle should be in a neutral position, leg vertical at a right angle to the sole of the foot.
In the lateral view, the knees should be in a neutral position, not flexed nor hyperextended.
In the lateral view, the LPHC should have the pelvis in a neutral position, not anteriorly (lumbar extension) or posteriorly (lumbar flexion) rotated.
In the lateral view, the shoulders should have a normal kyphotic curve, not excessively rounded.
In the lateral view, the head should be in a neutral position, not in excessive extension (“jutting” forward).
In the posterior view, the heels should be straight and parallel, not overly pronated.
In the posterior view, the knees should be in a neutral position, not adducted or abducted.
In the posterior view, the LPHC should have the pelvis level with both posterior superior iliac spines in the same transverse plane.
In the posterior view, the shoulders/scapulae should be level, not elevated or protracted (medial borders essentially parallel and approximately 3 to 4 inches apart).
In the posterior view, the head should be in a neutral position, neither tilted nor rotated.
Posture is often viewed as being static (or without movement).
Everyday posture is constantly changing to meet the demands placed on the kinetic chain.
The dynamic postural assessment should be performed after completing a static postural assessment.
The findings from the dynamic postural assessment should reinforce the observations made during the static postural assessment.
Faulty body alignments not revealed during the static postural assessment may be noted during the dynamic postural observations.
The dynamic postural assessment is often the quickest way to gain an overall impression of a client’s functional status.
Posture is also a dynamic quality.
These observations show postural distortion and potential overactive and underactive muscles in its naturally dynamic setting.
Movement observations should relate to basic functions such as squatting, pushing, pulling, and balancing.
Movement observations should provide crucial information about muscle and joint interplay.

The observation process should search for any imbalances in anatomy, physiology, or biomechanics that may decrease a client’s results and possibly lead to injury (both in and out of the fitness environment).
Incorporating a systematic assessment sequence is essential because most fitness professionals have limited time for observation.
The overhead squat assessment is designed to assess dynamic flexibility, core strength, balance, and overall neuromuscular control.
Evidence supports the use of transitional movement assessments such as the overhead squat test.
These assessments appear to be reliable and valid measures of lower extremity movement patterns when standard protocols are applied.
The overhead squat test has been shown to reflect lower extremity movement patterns during jump-landing tasks.
Knee valgus (knock-knees) during the overhead squat test is influenced by decreased hip abductor and hip external rotation strength.
Knee valgus (knock-knees) during the overhead squat test is also influenced by increased hip adductor activity and restricted ankle dorsiflexion.
The movement impairments observed during this transitional movement assessment may be the result of alterations in available joint motion, muscle activation, and overall neuromuscular control.
The client stands with the feet shoulders-width apart and pointed straight ahead.
The foot and ankle complex should be in a neutral position.
It is suggested that the assessment is performed with the shoes off to better view the foot and ankle complex.
The client raises his or her arms overhead, with elbows fully extended.
The upper arms should bisect the torso.
Instruct the client to squat to roughly the height of a chair seat and return to the starting position.
Repeat the movement for 5 repetitions, observing from each position (anterior and lateral).
View feet, ankles, and knees from the front.
The feet should remain straight with the knees tracking in line with the foot (2nd and 3rd toes).
View the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex, shoulder, and cervical complex from the side.
The tibia should remain in line with the torso while the arms also stay in line with the torso.
Do the feet flatten and/or turn out?
Do the knees move inward (adduct and internally rotate)?
Does the low back arch?
Does the torso lean forward excessively?
Do the arms fall forward?
The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings.
Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats.
A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
The client should squat to a comfortable level and return to the starting position.
The client should perform up to 5 repetitions before switching sides.
The knee should track in line with the foot (2nd and 3rd toes).
The knee should not move inward (adduct and internally rotate).
The lumbar and cervical spines should remain neutral while the shoulders stay level during the pushing assessment.
The client should stand with abdomen drawn inward, feet in a split stance and toes pointing forward during the pushing assessment.
The client should press handles forward and return to the starting position during the pushing assessment.
The client should perform up to 20 repetitions in a controlled fashion during the pushing assessment.
The low back should not arch during the pushing assessment.
The shoulders should not elevate during the pushing assessment.
The head should not migrate forward during the pushing assessment.
The purpose of the pulling assessment is to assess movement efficiency and potential muscle imbalances during pulling movements.
The client should stand with their abdomen drawn inward, feet shoulder-width apart, and toes pointing forward for the pulling assessment.
The client should pull handles toward their body and return to the starting position while maintaining a neutral lumbar and cervical spine and level shoulders during the pulling assessment.
The client should perform up to 20 repetitions of the pulling assessment in a controlled fashion.
Low back arching during the pulling assessment is a potential compensation.
Shoulder elevation during the pulling assessment is a potential compensation.
Head migration forward during the pulling assessment is a potential compensation.
Low back arches during the pulling assessment may indicate overactive hip flexors and underactive erector spinae and intrinsic core stabilizers.
Shoulder elevation during the pulling assessment may indicate overactive upper trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, and levator scapulae and underactive mid/lower trapezius.
Head migration forward during the pulling assessment may indicate overactive upper trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, and levator scapulae and underactive deep cervical flexors.
Posture is the alignment and function of all parts of the kinetic chain.
The main purpose of posture is to overcome constant forces placed on the body by maintaining structural efficiency.
The kinetic chain requires constant postural equilibrium.
Proper postural alignment puts the body in a state of optimal neuromuscular efficiency, allowing for proper joint mechanics and effective distribution of force throughout the kinetic chain.
Proper posture allows the body to produce high levels of functional strength.
Without proper posture, the body may degenerate or experience postural distortion patterns.
A dynamic postural observation examines basic movements and provides crucial information about how muscles and joints interact.
A dynamic postural observation searches for any imbalances in anatomy, physiology, or biomechanics.
Performance assessments can be used for clients looking to improve athletic performance.
Performance assessments measure upper extremity stability and muscular endurance, lower extremity agility, and overall strength.
Basic performance assessments include the push-up test, Davies test, shark skill test, bench press strength assessment, and squat strength assessment.
The push-up test measures muscular endurance of the upper body, primarily the pushing muscles.
The push-up assessment involves touching the chest to a partner's fist or the floor for 60 seconds or until exhaustion without compensating (arches low back, extends cervical spine).
A variation to the push-up assessment includes performing push-ups from a kneeling position.
The client or athlete should be able to perform more push-ups when reassessed.
The Davies Test measures upper extremity agility and stabilization.
The Davies Test may not be suitable for clients or athletes who lack shoulder stability.
The Davies Test procedure involves placing two pieces of tape on the floor, 36 inches apart.
The client assumes a push-up position with one hand on each piece of tape.
The client quickly moves their right hand to touch their left hand.
The client performs alternating touching on each side for 15 seconds.
The client repeats the Davies Test for three trials.
The Shark Skill Test assesses lower extremity agility and neuromuscular control.
The Shark Skill Test is a progression from the single-leg squat and may not be suitable for all individuals.
The client positions themselves in the center box of a grid, with hands on hips and standing on one leg.
The client hops to each box in a designated pattern, always returning to the center box.
The client performs one practice run through the boxes with each foot.
The client performs the Shark Skill Test twice with each foot (four times total).
The client's time is recorded.
0.10 seconds is added for each fault: non-hopping leg touches the ground, hands come off hips, foot goes into the wrong square, or foot does not return to the center square.
The Upper Extremity Strength Assessment: Bench Press is designed to estimate the one-rep maximum on overall upper body strength of the pressing musculature.
The Bench Press test can also be used to determine training intensities of the bench press.
The Bench Press test is considered an advanced assessment for strength-specific goals and may not be suitable for many clients.
Personal trainers should not perform the Bench Press test for clients with general fitness or weight-loss goals.
The client lies on a bench with feet pointed straight ahead and a neutral low back position.
The squat assessment is designed to estimate the one-repetition squat maximum and overall lower body strength.
This test can also be used to determine training intensities for the squat exercise.
This is considered an advanced assessment (for strength-specific goals) and may not be suitable for many clients.
Generally, personal trainers should not perform this assessment for clients with general fitness or weight-loss goals.
Feet should be shoulders-width apart, pointed straight ahead, and with knees in line with the toes.
The low back should be in a neutral position.
Instruct the client to warm up with a light resistance that can be easily performed for 8 to 10 repetitions.
Take a 1-minute rest.
Add 30 to 40 pounds (10–20% of initial load) and perform 3 to 5 repetitions.
Take a 2-minute rest.
Repeat steps 3 and 4 until the client achieves failure between 2 and 10 repetitions (3 to 5 repetitions for greater accuracy).
Use the one-rep maximum estimation chart in the appendix to calculate one-repetition max.
Brian is 24 years old.
Brian is a semiprofessional soccer player.
Brian travels often, competing in various soccer tournaments.
Brian likes to work out with weights three to four times per week.
Brian practices soccer five days per week.
Brian plays in an organized soccer game at least two times per week.
Brian had surgery for a torn anterior cruciate ligament in his left knee three years ago.
Brian has sprained his left ankle two times since his knee surgery.
Brian went through physical therapy for his last ankle sprain six months ago.
Brian was cleared to work out and play again six months ago.
Brian's knee and ankle have not been giving him any trouble, other than some occasional soreness after games and practice.
Brian recently went through a physical to begin playing again.
Brian's physician gave him a clean bill of health.
Brian wants to increase his overall performance by enhancing his flexibility, speed, cardiorespiratory efficiency, and leg strength.
Brian wants to decrease his risk of incurring other injuries.
Brian increased his body fat percentage after being out of soccer because of his injury.
Brian wants to lower his body fat percentage.

Recommended objective assessments for Brian include body fat measurement, the 3-minute step test or Rockport Walk Test, the overhead squat assessment, the single-leg squat assessment, the Davies test, the Shark skill test, and the lower extremity strength assessment: squat.
