Although some loss of physiologic, neuromuscular, and biomechanical capacity is an inevitability of the aging process, recent research has determined that these losses can be minimized by appropriate exercise interventions.
Osteopenia, or loss of bone density, is often related to the aging processes, particularly in women.
This increases the likelihood for fractures and other acute and chronic skeletal disorders such as osteoporosis.

Research has determined that properly administered programs requiring an elevated degree of load on the skeletal system such as those found in SAQ protocols are safe and effective in slowing and potentially reversing osteopenia in older adults.
Movement confidence and proficiency are essential in senior populations to aid in the prevention of falls and maintain activities of daily life.
A 10% loss of bone density at the hip can result in a 2.5 times greater risk for hip fracture.
Required for safe, effective movement often dissipate with age as a result of under-use.
SAQ-based programs have been found to increase coordinative ability and movement confidence, eliciting a decreased likelihood of falling or other movement-related injury.
Sarcopenia, or age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass, can be detrimental to maintaining functional capacity in older adults.
Resistance training as well as SAQ-based interventions has been found to help slow and reverse this process.
Interventions requiring increased speed of movement and rate of force production similar to those found in SAQ protocols are of particular relevance to slowing and reversing sarcopenia.
When designing an SAQ program for seniors, the personal trainer must take specialized care in ensuring safety for the participants.
Drills should focus around activities the individuals will need for daily life such as standing up from a chair, walking up stairs, navigating ground obstacles, etc.
The 5-10-5 drill consists of three 10-yard sprints.
The Modified Box Drill starts with the client at the middle cone.
For the Modified Box Drill, the trainer calls out a cone number and the client moves to that cone and returns to the middle cone.
The T-Drill consists of a sprint, a carioca, a side shuffle, a backpedal.
The Box Drill consists of a sprint, a side shuffle, and a backpedal.
The L.E.F.T. Drill consists of a sprint, a backpedal, two side shuffles, and two cariocas.
The client's name is John Smith.
The client's goal is fat loss.
The client is in the 1st stabilization endurance phase.

If speed, agility, and quickness training are to be used in the program, you will refer to Table 12.2 for the appropriate type of exercises, the appropriate number of exercises, and the appropriate acute variables specific to the phase of training your client will be working in (phases 1–5).
However, owing to its uniqueness and versatility, SAQ training can also be used as its own workout separate from an integrated training program.
In this instance, the volume of the program will typically be higher depending on the capabilities, goals, and conditioning level of the client.
Programming guidelines must be gauged on the total volume of training for all components in a workout.
A client’s core, balance, and reactive capabilities will determine the success and safety of the program.
Precise technique and kinetic chain control are required to minimize risk of injury.
Various speed ladders, cone drills, and other drills requiring the attributes of speed, agility, and quickness may be used in the programming.

Tjønna AE, Lee SJ, Rognmo Ø, et al. published a study titled 'Aerobic interval training versus continuous moderate exercise as a treatment for the metabolic syndrome: a pilot study' in Circulation in 2008.
Trembley A, Simaneau J, and Bouchard C published a study titled 'Impact of exercise intensity on body fatness and skeletal muscle metabolism' in Metabolism in 1994.

Jakicic JM, Wing RR, Butler BA, and Robertson RJ published a study titled 'Prescribing exercise in multiple short bouts versus one continuous bout: effects on adherence, cardiorespiratory fitness, and weight loss in overweight women' in Int J Obes Relat Metab Disord in 1995.

Liu-Ambrose TY, Khan KM, Eng JJ, Lord SR, Lentle B, and McKay HA published a study titled 'Both resistance and agility training reduce back pain and improve health-related quality of life in older women with low bone mass' in Osteoporos Int in 2005.
Bean J, Kiely D, Herman S, et al. published a study titled 'The relationship between leg power and physiological performance in mobility-limited people' in J Am Geriatr Soc in 2002.
Heinonen A, Kannus P, Sievänen H, et al. published a randomized controlled trial titled 'Effect of high-impact exercise on select risk factors for osteoporotic fractures' in Lancet in 1996.
Iwamoto J, Takeda T, and Ichimura S published a study titled 'Effect of exercise training and detraining on bone mineral density in postmenopausal women with osteoporosis' in J Orthop Sci in 2001.
Laroche DP, Knight CA, Dickie JL, Lussier M, and Roy SJ published a study titled 'Explosive force and fractionated reaction time in elderly low-and high-active women' in Med Sci Sports Exerc in 2007.

Liu-Ambrose T, Khan KM, Eng JJ, Janssen PA, Lord SR, and McKay HA published a randomized controlled trial titled 'Resistance and agility training reduce fall risk in women aged 75 to 85 with low bone mass: A 6-month randomized, controlled trial' in J Am Geriatr Soc in 2004.

Sundstrup E, Jakobsen MD, Andersen JL, et al. published a study titled 'Muscle function and postural balance in lifelong trained male footballers compared with sedentary men and youngsters' in Scand J Med Sci Sports in 2010.

Newton RU, Hakkinen K, Hakkinen A, McCormick M, Volek J, and Kraemer WJ published a study titled 'Mixed-methods resistance training increases power and strength of young and older men' in Med Sci Sports Exerc in 2002.
Porter MM published a study titled 'Power training for older adults' in Appl Physiol Nutr Metab in 2006.

Bean JF, Herman S, Kiely DK, et al. published a pilot study titled 'Increased velocity exercise specific to task (InVEST) training: a pilot study exploring effects on leg power, balance, and mobility in community-dwelling older women' in J Am Geriatr Soc in 2004.

Klotzbuecher CM, Ross PD, Landsman PB, Abbott TA 3rd, and Berger M published a study titled 'Patients with prior fractures have an increased risk of future fractures: A summary of the literature and statistical synthesis' in J Bone Miner Res in 2000.
The primary goal of most exercise training programs is adaptation.
Resistance training has been shown to produce a variety of desirable effects.
The optimal state for the human movement system is one of physiologic balance or homeostasis.
The general adaptation syndrome (GAS) is a term used to describe how the body responds and adapts to stress.

Exercise, including resistance training, can be considered a good form of stress called ‘eustress’ that over time allows the human movement system to adapt and thus be able to maintain homeostatic states under a variety of conditions.
For adaptation to occur, the body must be confronted with a stressor or some form of stress that creates the need for a response.
Hans Selye, a Canadian physician, first described the general pattern of adaptation.
Selye outlined three stages of response to stress: alarm reaction, resistance development, and exhaustion.
The alarm reaction is the initial reaction to a stressor.
During the initial sessions of resistance training programs, the body is forced to try and adapt to increased amounts of force on bones, joints, muscles, connective tissues, and the nervous system.
During the alarm stage of resistance training, numerous physiological responses occur, including an increase in oxygen and blood supply as well as neural recruitment to the working muscles.
Delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is pain or discomfort often felt 24 to 72 hours after intense exercise or unaccustomed physical activity.
Minimizing DOMS involves starting a progressive training program at a low intensity and introducing overload gradually.
During the resistance development stage, the body increases its functional capacity to adapt to the stressor.
After repeated training sessions, the human movement system will increase its capability to efficiently recruit muscle fibers and distribute oxygen and blood to the proper areas in the body.
Once adaptation has occurred, the body will require increased stress or overload to produce a new response and a higher level of fitness.
Performance will continue to improve until some new performance plateau is reached and will be maintained if training is maintained.
Overtraining syndrome commonly occurs in athletes or fitness enthusiasts who are training beyond the body’s ability to recover.
When an individual is performing excessive amounts of exercise without proper rest and recovery, there may be some harmful side effects.

Some harmful side effects of overtraining may include decreased performance, fatigue, altered hormonal states, poor sleeping patterns, reproductive disorders, decreased immunity, loss of appetite, and mood disturbances.
Prolonged stress or intolerable amounts of stress can lead to exhaustion or distress.
When a stressor is too much for any one of the physiologic systems to handle, it causes a breakdown or injury such as stress fractures, muscle strains, joint pain, or emotional fatigue.
Many of these types of injuries can lead to the initiation of the cumulative injury cycle.
Avoiding the pitfalls of the exhaustion stage is one of the main reasons for using the OPT model (a systematic, progressive training program) that is based on science and proven application.

Resistance training, as well as other forms of training, must be cycled through different stages that increase stress placed on the human movement system, but also allow for sufficient rest and recuperation periods.
The term used to describe this approach, in which a training program is divided into smaller, progressive stages, is periodization.

If the resistance is continually increased with the intention of stressing specific muscles or muscle groups to produce an increase in size or strength, it can lead to injury of the muscle, joint, or connective tissue, especially if the resistance is added too quickly or inadequate rest and recovery periods are not planned for.
Training-related injuries occur more often to connective tissue (such as ligaments and tendons) than muscles because connective tissues lack blood supply.
Different tissues in the body (muscle fibers versus connective tissue) each have their own adaptive potential to stresses.
Training programs should provide a variety of intensities and stresses to optimize the adaptation of each tissue to ensure the best possible results.
Adaptation can be more specifically applied to certain aspects of the human movement system depending on the training technique(s) used, which is the basis of the principle of specificity.
Periodization is the division of a training program into smaller, progressive stages.
Exhaustion is prolonged stress or stress that is intolerable and will produce exhaustion or distress to the system.
The principle of specificity, often referred to as the SAID (specific adaptation to imposed demands) principle, states that the body will specifically adapt to the type of demand placed on it.
If someone repeatedly lifts heavy weights, that person will produce higher levels of maximal strength.
If a person repeatedly lifts lighter weights for many repetitions, that person will develop higher levels of muscular endurance.
Training programs should reflect the desired outcome(s).
Type I or slow-twitch muscle fibers are smaller in diameter, slower to produce maximal tension, and more resistant to fatigue.
Type I fibers are important for muscles that need to produce long-term contractions necessary for stabilization, endurance, and postural control.
Type II or fast-twitch muscle fibers are larger in size, quick to produce maximal tension, and fatigue more quickly than type I fibers.
These fibers are important for muscles producing movements requiring force and power such as performing a sprint.
To train with higher intensities, proper postural stabilization is required.
Therefore, tissues need to be trained differently to prepare them for higher levels of training.
This is the specific purpose behind periodization and the OPT model.
The degree of adaptation that occurs during training is directly related to the mechanical, neuromuscular, and metabolic specificity of the training program.
To effectively achieve program goals for clients, personal trainers need to consistently evaluate the need to manipulate the exercise routine to meet actual training goals.
Remember, the body can only adapt if it has a reason to adapt.
Mechanical specificity refers to the weight and movements placed on the body.
To develop muscular endurance of the legs, light weights and high repetitions should be used when performing leg-related exercises.
To develop maximal strength in the chest, heavy weights must be used during chest-related exercises.
Neuromuscular specificity refers to the speed of contraction and exercise selection.
To develop higher levels of stability while pushing, chest exercises should be performed with controlled, unstable exercises at slower speeds.
To develop higher levels of strength, exercises should be performed in more stable environments with heavier loads to place more emphasis on the prime movers.
To develop higher levels of power, low-weight, high-velocity contractions must be performed in a plyometric manner.
Metabolic specificity refers to the energy demand placed on the body.
To develop endurance, training will require prolonged bouts of exercise with minimal rest periods between sets.
Endurance training primarily uses aerobic pathways to supply energy for the body.
To develop maximal strength or power, training will require longer rest periods so the intensity of each bout of exercise remains high.
Energy will be supplied primarily via the anaerobic pathways.
A client’s training program should be designed to meet the specific demands of their daily life and health and wellness goals.
The body burns more calories when movements are performed while standing versus a seated or lying position and using moderate weights.
The body burns more calories when more muscles are being used for longer periods in controlled, unstable environments.
The body burns more calories when rest periods are short to minimize full recuperation.
A training program for weight loss should have the client perform the majority of exercises while standing and using moderate weights.
The client should also recruit and use as many muscles as possible during each exercise and carefully monitor rest periods for greater caloric expenditure.
A well-designed, integrated training program produces optimal levels of flexibility, endurance, neuromuscular control, alterations in body composition, strength, and power.
Adaptive programs must include carefully and methodically planned (periodization) cycles through different stages, which allows for sufficient rest and recuperation to avoid injury.

Adaptive programs have several benefits, including improved cardiovascular efficiency, beneficial endocrine (hormone) and serum lipid (cholesterol) adaptations, increased bone density, and increased metabolic efficiency (metabolism).
Adaptive programs have several physical benefits, including increased tissue (muscles, tendons, ligaments) tensile strength, increased cross-sectional area of muscle fibers, and decreased body fat.
Adaptive programs have several performance benefits, including increased neuromuscular control (coordination), increased endurance, increased strength, and increased power.
The type of training adaptation and the goals of the client should determine the design of the training program.
There are different types of strength and different systems of strength training that can be used to create a more individualized and systematic program for clients.
Different tissues in the body each respond to different stresses as seen in the principle of specificity or specific adaptation to imposed demands (SAID principle).
Training programs should provide a variety of intensities and stresses to optimize the adaptation of each tissue to ensure the best possible results.
The degree of adaptation that occurs during training is directly related to the mechanical, neuromuscular, and metabolic specificity of the training program.
Resistance training programs are designed to produce changes that result in various adaptations.

Whether the goal is to increase muscle endurance, strength, hypertrophy, or power or to reduce body fat and improve overall health, the use of resistance training is an important component of any fitness program.

As clients develop greater strength and endurance, they can train for longer periods before reaching the exhaustion stage (general adaptation syndrome), which leads to greater degrees of change and adaptation realized over time.
The main adaptations that occur from resistance training include stabilization, muscular endurance, hypertrophy, strength, and power.
Stabilization is the human movement system’s ability to provide optimal dynamic joint support to maintain correct posture during all movements.
Proper exercise technique involves getting the right muscles to fire with the right amount of force, in the proper plane of motion, and at the right time.
High levels of muscular endurance are required for optimal recruitment of prime movers to increase concentric force production and reduce eccentric force.
Repeatedly training with controlled, unstable exercises increases the body’s ability to stabilize and balance itself.
If training is not performed with controlled unstable exercises, clients will not gain the same level of stability and may even worsen.
Research shows that improper stabilization can negatively affect a muscle’s force production.

Stability is an important training adaptation because it increases the ability of the kinetic chain to stabilize the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex and joints during movement to allow the arms and legs to work more efficiently.
Muscular endurance is the ability to produce and maintain force production for prolonged periods of time.
Improving muscular endurance is an integral component of all fitness programs.
Developing muscular endurance helps to increase core and joint stabilization, which is the foundation on which hypertrophy, strength, and power are built.
Training for muscular endurance of the core focuses on the recruitment of muscles responsible for postural stability, namely, type I muscle fibers.
Research has shown that resistance training protocols using high repetitions are the most effective way to improve muscular endurance.
A periodization training program can enhance local muscular endurance.
After an initial training effect in previously untrained individuals, multiple sets of periodized training may prove superior to single-set training for improving muscle endurance.

Campos et al. (25) found that higher repetitions (2 sets of 20–28 repetitions with 1-minute rest periods beginning at 2 days/week) increased local muscle endurance and hypertrophy in untrained men after an 8-week training program.

Marx et al. (26) found that after the initial 12 weeks of training, multiple sets of up to 15 repetitions, 4 times a week for 6 months, resulted in a threefold decrease in body fat and an increase in local muscle endurance, as well as a significant increase in lean body mass.
Muscular hypertrophy is the enlargement of skeletal muscle fibers in response to being recruited to develop increased levels of tension, as seen in resistance training.
Muscle hypertrophy is characterized by an increase in the cross-sectional area of individual muscle fibers resulting from an increase in myofibril proteins (myofilaments).

Although the visible signs of hypertrophy may not be apparent for many weeks (4 to 8 weeks) in an untrained client, the process begins in the early stages of training, regardless of the intensity of training used.
Resistance training protocols that use low to intermediate repetition ranges with progressive overload lead to muscular hypertrophy.
Structured progressive resistance training programs using multiple sets help to increase muscular hypertrophy in both younger and older men and women alike.
Kraemer et al. (4) demonstrated that 24 weeks of training 3 days per week with 3 sets of 8–12 repetitions per exercise improved muscle hypertrophy and body composition.
Progressive resistance training programs using moderate to low repetition protocols with progressively higher loads will result in increased hypertrophy in older adults and men and women.
Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension in the muscles and connective tissues that pull on the bones to overcome an external force.
Whether the external force demands stability, endurance, maximal strength, or power, internal tension within the muscles leads to force production.
The degree of internal tension produced is the result of strength adaptations.
The specific form of strength or internal tension produced from training is based on the type and intensity of training used by the client.
Resistance training programs have traditionally focused on developing maximal strength in individual muscles, emphasizing one plane of motion (typically the sagittal plane).
All muscles function eccentrically, isometrically, and concentrically in all three planes of motion (sagittal, frontal, and transverse) at different speeds.
Training programs should be designed using a progressive approach that emphasizes the appropriate exercise selection, all muscle actions, and repetition tempos.
Muscle operates under the control of the central nervous system, so strength needs to be thought of not as a function of muscle, but as a result of activating the neuromuscular system.
Strength gains can occur rapidly in beginning clients and can increase with a structured, progressive resistance training program.
One factor in increased strength is an increase in the number of motor units recruited, especially early in a training program.

Using heavier loads increases the neural demand and recruitment of more muscle fibers until a recruitment plateau is reached, after which further increases in strength are a result of fiber hypertrophy.

Strength cannot be thought of in isolation. Strength is built on the foundation of stabilization requiring muscles, tendons, and ligaments to be prepared for the load that will be required to increase strength beyond the initial stages of training.

Stabilization training is designed with the characteristics of type I slow-twitch muscle fibers in mind (slow-contracting, low tension output, and resistant to fatigue), while strength training is designed to match the characteristics of type II muscle fibers (quick-contracting, high tension output, prone to fatigue).
Acute variables (sets, reps, intensities, etc.) are manipulated to take advantage of the specific characteristics of each fiber type.
The majority of strength increases will occur during the first 12 weeks of resistance training from increased neural recruitment and muscle hypertrophy.
Intermediate and advanced lifters will find it necessary to carry out a more demanding program in terms of training volume and intensity by following a sound periodized schedule.
Power is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest possible force in the shortest possible time.
This is represented by the simple equation of force multiplied by velocity.
Power adaptations build on stabilization and strength adaptations and then apply them at more realistic speeds and forces seen in everyday life and sporting activities.
The focus of power-resistance training is getting the neuromuscular system to generate force as quickly as possible (rate of force production).
An increase in either force or velocity will produce an increase in power.
Training for power can be achieved by increasing the weight (force), as seen in the strength adaptations, or increasing the speed with which weight is moved (velocity).
Power training allows for increased rate of force production by increasing the number of motor units recruited and the speed of contraction.

The general adaptation syndrome and principle of specificity dictate that to maximize training for muscle fiber recruitment and rate of activation, both heavy and light loads must be moved as fast as possible in a controlled fashion.

Early isokinetic work underscored the importance of speed of exercise, showing that training performed at high speeds led to better performance at the training speed and all movement speeds below the training speed.
Resistance training programs produce physiologic changes that result in a variety of strength adaptations.
Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension to overcome an external force.

Traditionally, resistance training programs focused on developing maximal strength in individual muscles, whereas contemporary training programs emphasize appropriate exercise selection, all muscle actions, multiple planes of motion, and repetition tempos.
Training adaptations can be divided into stabilization, muscular endurance, hypertrophy, strength, and power.
Stabilization adaptations should be the starting point for clients new to resistance training.
Stabilization adaptations are best achieved using high repetitions with low to moderate volume and low to moderate intensity in a postural position that challenges the stability of the body.
Muscular endurance is the ability to produce and maintain force production for prolonged periods of time.
Research has shown that resistance training protocols using high repetitions are the most effective way to improve muscular endurance.
Muscular hypertrophy is the enlargement of skeletal muscle fibers in response to increased volumes of tension resulting from resistance training.
Resistance training protocols that use low to intermediate repetition ranges with progressive overload lead to muscular hypertrophy.
Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension (in the muscles and connective tissues that pull on the bones) to overcome an external force.
The specific form of strength produced is based on the type and intensity of training used by the client (principle of specificity).
Generally, strength adaptations should use low to moderate repetition routines with moderate to high volume and moderate to high intensity.
Heavier weights and higher volumes of training are used to improve the function of motor units, while placing stress on the muscles to increase size or strength.
Power is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest possible force in the shortest possible time.
An increase in either force (weight) or velocity (speed with which weight is moved) will produce an increase in power.
To maximize training for this adaptation, both heavy and light loads must be moved as fast and as controlled as possible.

Research has shown that following a systematic, integrated training program and manipulating key training variables achieve optimal gains in strength, neuromuscular efficiency, hypertrophy, and performance.
There are numerous training systems that can be used to structure resistance training programs for a variety of effects.
The Single-Set System uses 1 set per exercise.
Single-set workouts are usually recommended to be performed two times per week to promote sufficient development and maintenance of muscle mass.
Multiple-set training is promoted as being more beneficial for strength and hypertrophy gains in advanced clients.
The single-set system has been shown to be just as beneficial for beginning-level clients.
Single-set training systems are often negatively perceived for not providing enough stimuli for adaptation.
Most beginning clients could follow a single-set program to allow for proper adaptive responses of the connective tissue and nervous system before engaging in more rigorous training systems.
By encouraging clients to avoid lifting more than they can handle, synergistic dominance (synergists overcompensating for weak prime movers) and injury can be avoided.
The Multiple-Set System consists of performing multiple numbers of sets for each exercise.
The resistance (load), sets, and repetitions performed are selected according to the goals and needs of the client.
Multiple-set training can be appropriate for both novice and advanced clients, but has been shown to be superior to single-set training for more advanced clients.
The increased volume (sets, reps, and intensity) is necessary for further improvement, but must be administered appropriately to avoid overtraining.
The Pyramid System involves a progressive or regressive step approach that either increases weight with each set or decreases weight with each set.

In the light-to-heavy system, the individual typically performs 10 to 12 repetitions with a light load and increases the resistance for each following set, until the individual can perform 1 to 2 repetitions, usually in 4 to 6 sets.
This system can easily be used for workouts that involve only 2 to 4 sets or higher repetition schemes (12 to 20 repetitions).

The heavy-to-light system works in the opposite direction, in which the individual begins with a heavy load (after a sufficient warm-up) for 1 to 2 repetitions, then decreases the load and increases the repetitions for 4 to 6 sets.
The Superset System uses two exercises performed in rapid succession of one another.
The first variation includes performing two exercises for the same muscle group back to back.
An individual may perform the bench press exercise immediately followed by push-ups to fatigue the chest musculature.
Completing two exercises in this manner will improve muscular endurance and hypertrophy because the volume of work performed is relatively high.
This style of supersets can use two, three (a tri-set), or more exercises (a giant set) for the target muscle group.
The greater the number of exercises used, the greater the degree of fatigue experienced and demands on muscular endurance.
The second variation of training consists of performing two exercises back to back that involve antagonist muscle groups (e.g., chest and back or quadriceps and hamstring complex).
Performing supersets in this manner allows a significant load to be placed on the target muscle during each set because while the agonist is working, the antagonist is recovering, and vice versa.
Supersetting typically involves sets of 8 to 12 repetitions with no rest between sets or exercises, however, any number of repetitions can be used.
The superset system is popular among bodybuilders and may be beneficial for muscular hypertrophy and muscular endurance.
A drop-set is a resistance training system that is popular among bodybuilders.
It is a technique that allows a client to continue a set past the point at which it would usually terminate.
Drop-sets involve performing a set to failure, then removing a small percentage of the load (5–20%) and continuing with the set, completing a small number of repetitions (usually 2–4).
This procedure can be repeated several times (typically 2 to 3 drops per set).
A set to failure followed by three successive load decrements performed with no rest would be referred to as a triple drop.
Drop-sets are considered an advanced form of resistance training suitable for experienced lifters.
The circuit-training system consists of a series of exercises that an individual performs one after the other, with minimal rest between each exercise.

The typical acute variables for a circuit-training program include a low to moderate number of sets (1–3), with moderate to high repetitions (8–20) and short rest periods (15–60 seconds) between exercises; however, these variables can be manipulated to enhance the desired effect.
Circuit training is a great training system for individuals with limited time and for those who want to alter body composition.
The peripheral heart action system is another variation of circuit training that alternates upper body and lower body exercises throughout the circuit.
This system of training distributes blood flow between the upper and lower extremities, potentially improving circulation.
The number of exercises per sequence varies with the program’s goal.
The individual performs 8 to 20 repetitions per exercise, depending on the desired adaptation and phase of training they are using in the OPT model.
This system is very beneficial for incorporating an integrated, multidimensional program and for altering body composition.
A split-routine system involves breaking the body up into parts to be trained on separate days.
Many bodybuilders and mass-dominant and strength athletes (football, shot put, etc.) use the split-routine system.
Bodybuilders typically perform numerous exercises on the same day for the same body part to bring about optimal muscular hypertrophy.
By breaking up the body into parts that can be trained on different days, more work can be performed for the allotted time per workout.
The 5-Day Monday Chest workout schedule includes chest exercises on Monday, legs exercises on Tuesday, back exercises on Wednesday, shoulders exercises on Thursday, and arms exercises on Friday.

The 6-Day Monday and Friday workout schedule includes chest, shoulders, and triceps exercises on Monday and Friday, legs exercises on Tuesday and Saturday, and back and biceps exercises on Wednesday and Sunday.
Vertical loading is a resistance training system used by NASM and follows the OPT model.
The OPT template for resistance training includes total body, chest, back, shoulders, biceps, triceps, and legs exercises.
In a vertically loaded workout, the client performs the first exercise (total body) for the required repetitions and then moves to the next exercise (chest) for the next set of repetitions.
The client continues to alternate exercises in a vertical loading workout until all exercises have been completed.
Once completed, the client starts back at the first exercise (total body) and runs through the exercises again for the desired amount of sets.
A vertically loaded workout can be performed in a circuit style by minimizing the rest periods between exercises.
Vertical loading can be beneficial for allowing maximal recovery to each body part while minimizing the amount of time wasted on rest.
Horizontal loading refers to performing all sets of an exercise or body part before moving on to the next exercise or body part.
For example, if performing three sets of a chest exercise and three sets of a back exercise, the client would perform all three sets of the chest exercise before moving on to the back exercise.
Horizontal loading is the method most commonly used in health club environments and is appropriate for maximal strength and power training when longer rest periods are required between sets.
The drawback to the horizontal loading system is the amount of time typically spent resting, which can often be more time than the actual workout itself.
Horizontal loading can be a metabolic progression if rest periods are monitored and limited to 30 to 90 seconds between sets.
If the same muscle groups are forced to work with minimal recovery, it can lead to faster development of metabolic and hypertrophy-related adaptations in the muscle.
The single-set system consists of performing one set of each exercise, usually of 8 to 12 repetitions.
The single-set system has been shown to be beneficial for strength and hypertrophy gains in beginning clients.
The multiple-set system of training consists of performing a multiple number of sets for each exercise, with resistance, sets, and repetitions adjusted according to the goals and needs of the client.
The multiple-set system is superior to single-set training for more advanced clients.
The pyramid system involves a progressive or regressive step approach that either increases weight with each set or decreases weight with each set.
The superset system uses two exercises performed in rapid succession of one another.
The superset system is popular among bodybuilders for muscular hypertrophy and muscular endurance.
The circuit-training system programs consist of a series of exercises that an individual performs one immediately after the other, with minimal rest.
The circuit-training system is good for clients with limited time and those who want to alter body composition.
The peripheral heart action system is another variation of circuit training that alternates upper body and lower body exercises (of varied numbers) throughout the circuit.
A split-routine system involves breaking the body up into parts to be trained on separate days so that more work can be performed for the allotted time per workout.
When training each body part more than once a week, recovery period, volume, and intensity should be accounted for.
Vertical loading and horizontal loading progress a workout vertically or horizontally down the template.
In a vertically loaded workout, the client would perform each exercise until all exercises have been completed and then run through the exercises again for the desired amount of sets.

In a vertically loaded workout, the client can also perform the exercises in a circuit style, by minimizing the rest periods between exercises to allow maximal recovery to each body part and minimize the amount of time wasted on rest.
In a horizontally loaded workout, the client would perform all sets of an exercise or body part before moving on to the next exercise or body part.
Begin with both feet shoulders-width apart pointing straight ahead and knees over the second and third toes.
Position the ball in the low-back region as shown.
Lower into a squat position, keeping lower extremities in proper alignment.
Stand to a fully upright position while contracting the gluteal muscles and quadriceps.
Once stabilized, curl and press the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended.
Slowly return to the start position and repeat.
Decrease the range of motion.
Perform alternating-arm exercises.
Perform one-arm exercises.
Perform single-leg exercises.
When performing any form of a ball squat, try to use the ball to guide one through the squatting motion (sitting into a chair) versus relying on the ball for support (leaning back on the ball).
Stand in front of a box with feet shoulders-width apart.
Step onto the box with one leg, keeping foot and knee pointed straight ahead.
Push through the heel and stand up straight, balancing on one leg.
Flex the other leg at the hip and knee.
Once balance has been established, curl and press the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended.
Slowly return the dumbbells to the starting position.
Return the opposite leg to the ground and step off the box.
Repeat on the other leg.
Omit the balance component.
Perform exercises in the frontal plane.
Perform exercises in the transverse plane.
When pressing overhead, make sure the low back does not arch. This may indicate tightness of the latissimus dorsi and weakness of the intrinsic core stabilizers.
The lunge to two-arm dumbbell press exercise begins with feet shoulder-width apart and dumbbells held at chest level with palms facing the body.

During the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press, the individual lunges forward, coming to a stabilized position with the front foot pointing straight ahead and the knee directly over the second and third toes.
Both knees should be bent at a 90-degree angle during the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press, with the front foot flat on the ground and the back foot having the heel lifted off the ground.
The individual drives off the front foot and back into a standing position during the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press.
The dumbbells are pressed overhead until the arms are fully extended during the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press.
When performing any squatting or lunging motion, the foot should stay straight and the knees should stay in line with the toes to ensure proper joint mechanics and optimal force generation.
The squat, curl, to two-arm press exercise begins with feet shoulder-width apart.
The individual holds two dumbbells at shoulder level during the squat, curl, to two-arm press exercise.
The two-arm push press exercise begins with feet shoulder-width apart and dumbbells held at shoulder level.
The individual quickly drives the dumbbells up as if doing a shoulder press during the two-arm push press exercise.

The individual drives the legs into a stagger stance position during the two-arm push press exercise, with the back leg in triple extension (plantar flexion, knee extension, hip extension) and the front leg bent slightly.
The individual maintains optimal alignment on the return to the starting position during the two-arm push press exercise.
One must establish proper stability and prime mover strength before progressing to power exercises.
The squat begins with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead, with knees over the second and third toes.
The individual performs a squat as low as can be safely controlled, keeping lower extremities in proper alignment during the squat.
The individual activates gluteal muscles and stands to a fully upright position before any compensation occurs during the squat.
Once stabilized, the individual curls and presses the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended with palms facing away during the squat.
The individual slowly returns the dumbbells to the start position and repeats during the squat.
The barbell clean exercise begins with the individual squatting down next to the bar, keeping feet flat on the floor.
The individual grasps the bar fully extended slightly wider than shoulder-width apart during the barbell clean exercise.
The shoulders should be positioned over or slightly ahead of the bar, and the pelvis should be in a neutral position during the barbell clean exercise.
The individual initiates the first pull by extending knees and hips, lifting the bar straight up (keeping the bar close to the shins) with elbows extended during the barbell clean exercise.
The individual continues to move the bar upward by explosively extending hips, knees, and ankles (plantarflexion) in a jumping motion during the barbell clean exercise.
At maximal plantarflexion, the individual elevates shoulders and flexes and pulls with the arms during the barbell clean exercise.
The individual pulls the bar as high as possible before rotating elbows under the bar during the barbell clean exercise.
The individual hyperextends wrists as the elbows move under the bar during the barbell clean exercise.
The individual points elbows forward during the barbell clean exercise.
The individual racks the bar across the front of the shoulders, keeping the torso erect during the barbell clean exercise.
The individual flexes hips and knees to absorb the weight of the bar during the barbell clean exercise.
The Ball Dumbbell Chest Press involves lying on a stability ball in a bridge position with feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointing straight ahead.
During the Ball Dumbbell Chest Press, dumbbells are held at chest level and pressed straight up and together by extending elbows and contracting the chest.
The Ball Dumbbell Chest Press can be progressed to alternating-arm and single-arm variations.
To ensure proper alignment during the Ball Dumbbell Chest Press, the ears, shoulders, hips, and knees should be in line.
The Push-Up involves beginning in a push-up position with the navel drawn in and gluteal muscles contracted.
During a Push-Up, the pelvis should be kept in a neutral position while lowering the body toward the ground by flexing elbows and retracting and depressing shoulder blades.
The Push-Up can be progressed to lower extremities on a ball, hands on medicine balls, and hands on a stability ball.
The Push-Up can be regressed to on knees, hands on a bench with feet on the floor, and hands on a wall with feet on the floor.
A common compensation during a Push-Up is low back arching, indicating weak intrinsic core stabilizers.
The Flat Dumbbell Chest Press involves lying on a flat bench with knees bent and feet flat on the floor.
During the Flat Dumbbell Chest Press, dumbbells are held at chest level and pressed straight up and together by extending elbows and contracting the chest.
The Flat Dumbbell Chest Press involves slowly returning dumbbells toward the body by flexing elbows and allowing shoulders to retract and depress.
When performing chest presses, the range of motion at the shoulder joint is determined by the load and tissue extensibility.
The Barbell Bench Press involves lying on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and toes pointing straight ahead.
During the Barbell Bench Press, a barbell is held with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart.
The Barbell Bench Press involves slowly lowering the bar toward the chest by flexing elbows while avoiding back arching or head jutting.
The Barbell Bench Press involves pressing the bar back up by extending arms and contracting the chest until elbows are fully extended.
The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass involves standing facing a wall or partner in a squared stance position.
During the Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass, a medicine ball (5 to 10% of body weight) is held with both hands at chest level.
The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass involves pushing and releasing the ball straight ahead as hard as possible by extending elbows and contracting the chest.
The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass can be performed using tubing or cable if a medicine ball is unavailable.
The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass should be performed quickly and under control without compensation.
The Rotation Chest Pass exercise involves standing at a 90-degree angle from a wall or partner.
In the Rotation Chest Pass, a medicine ball weighing 5 to 10% of body weight is held with both hands at chest level with elbows flexed.
The Rotation Chest Pass movement involves using abdominal, hip, and gluteal muscles to rotate the body quickly and explosively toward the wall.
During the Rotation Chest Pass, the back leg pivots and goes into triple extension (plantarflexion, knee extension, hip extension) as the body turns.
The upper body pushes the medicine ball using the back arm to extend and apply force during the Rotation Chest Pass.
The Rotation Chest Pass should be performed quickly and under control.
The Standing Cable Row exercise involves standing facing a cable machine with feet pointing straight ahead.
In the Standing Cable Row, the cables are held with arms extended at chest level.
The Standing Cable Row movement involves rowing the cable by flexing elbows, retracting and depressing the shoulder blades with knees slightly flexed.
During the Standing Cable Row, the head should not jut forward and the shoulders should not elevate.
The Standing Cable Row progression options include seated, two-legs, alternating-arm, two-legs, one-arm, single-leg, two-arms, and single-leg, one-arm.
When performing rows, initiate the movement by retracting and depressing the shoulder blades (scapulae) and avoid elevating the shoulders.
The Ball Dumbbell Row exercise begins in a prone position with a stability ball under the abdomen.
In the Ball Dumbbell Row, feet are pointed down and legs are completely straight.
Dumbbells are held in each hand and extended in front of the body during the Ball Dumbbell Row.
The Ball Dumbbell Row movement involves contracting gluteal muscles and quadriceps and rowing the dumbbells by flexing elbows and retracting and depressing shoulder blades.
The Ball Dumbbell Row progression options include kneeling over the ball and alternating-arm and one-arm.
Performing exercises in a prone position can be uncomfortable, so for overweight individuals, seated or standing positions may be more appropriate.
The seated cable row exercise involves sitting facing a cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead.
During the seated cable row, the individual holds the cables with arms extended and rows the cable by flexing elbows and pulling the handles toward the trunk.
In the seated cable row, the thumbs should be brought toward the armpits while keeping the shoulder blades retracted and depressed.
The head should not jut forward during the seated cable row.
To increase the effectiveness of the seated cable row and decrease the risk of injury, the torso should be kept stationary throughout the exercise.
The seated lat pulldown exercise involves sitting upright with feet on the floor pointing straight ahead.
During the seated lat pulldown, the handles are pulled toward the body by flexing elbows and depressing the shoulder blades.
The individual should not arch back, allow the head to jut forward, or elevate the shoulders during the seated lat pulldown.
The seated lat pulldown should be performed with the bar passing in front of the face approximately to shoulder height if using a bar instead of cables.
Performing lat pulldowns with a bar behind the neck is not advised as it places stress on the shoulder joints and cervical spine.
The medicine ball pullover throw exercise involves placing a stability ball under the low back, bending knees at a 90-degree angle, and keeping feet flat with toes pointing straight ahead.
The individual holds a medicine ball (5 to 10% of body weight) overhead with both hands, with arms extended, for the medicine ball pullover throw.
The medicine ball pullover throw is performed by using the abdominals to quickly crunch forward and throw the medicine ball straight ahead while keeping elbows fairly straight.
As the medicine ball releases during the medicine ball pullover throw, the arms continue pulling through to the sides of the body.
The chin should be tucked throughout the medicine ball pullover throw exercise.
The soccer throw exercise involves standing holding a medicine ball (5 to 10% of body weight) overhead.
The soccer throw is performed by quickly throwing the medicine ball toward the floor, allowing the arms to follow through.
Optimal extensibility through the latissimus dorsi musculature is important before performing back-power exercises to decrease stress to the shoulder and low back.
Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption involves holding dumbbells at the side with palms facing the side of the body.
During Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption, raise one foot off the floor.
In Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption, raise both arms, thumbs up, at a 45-degree angle in front of the body until hands reach approximately eye level.
Keep shoulder blades retracted and depressed throughout Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption.
Do not allow the back to arch during Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption.
Slowly return arms back to the sides of the body and repeat Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption.
Regression options for Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption include two-legs and seated.
Progression options for Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption include single-leg, alternating-arm, single-leg, single-arm, and proprioceptive modalities.
Performing shoulder exercises in the scapular plane decreases the risk of the supraspinatus muscle becoming impinged between the head of the humerus and the coracoacromial arch of the scapula.
Seated Stability Ball Military Press involves sitting on a stability ball with toes pointed straight ahead and feet hips-width apart.
Hold dumbbells at shoulder level during Seated Stability Ball Military Press.
Press the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended with palms facing away during Seated Stability Ball Military Press.
Slowly return dumbbells to the start position and repeat Seated Stability Ball Military Press.
Regression for Seated Stability Ball Military Press is seated on a bench.
Progression options for Seated Stability Ball Military Press include alternating-arm, one-arm, and standing.
Performing exercises on a stability ball can be uncomfortable for some people.
A health and fitness professional may need to hold the ball while an individual performs the exercise to provide support during Seated Stability Ball Military Press.
Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press involves sitting on a bench with feet flat on the floor and toes pointing straight ahead.
Hold dumbbells in each hand during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press.
Press arms directly overhead by extending elbows during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press.
Do not allow the head to jut forward or the back to arch during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press.
Slowly return to the starting position and repeat Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press.
When performing overhead presses, make sure the cervical spine stays neutral (head drawn back) during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press.
Do not allow the head to migrate forward during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press as this places excessive stress on the posterior neck muscles and cervical spine.
The Seated Shoulder Press Machine exercise requires the user to sit with feet straight ahead, make adjustments for a proper fit, and select the desired weight.
During the Seated Shoulder Press Machine exercise, the user should keep their chin tucked.

The movement phase of the Seated Shoulder Press Machine exercise involves pressing the weight overhead until the arms are fully extended, holding the position, and then slowly returning the weight to the original position by flexing the elbows and allowing the shoulder blades to retract and depress.
The Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise requires the user to stand facing a wall or partner with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, and toes pointing straight ahead.
For the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise, the user should hold a medicine ball weighing between 5 and 10% of their body weight.

The movement phase of the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise involves tossing the ball against a wall or to a partner with an underhand motion, using a scooping motion to catch the ball, and repeating as quickly as can be controlled.
The Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise can be performed continuously to one side or by alternating sides.
If a partner is unavailable for the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise, the user can perform it by tossing the medicine ball against a wall.
The Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise requires the user to stand with their back to a wall in a semi-squat position, holding a medicine ball as shown.

The movement phase of the Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise involves jumping off the ground explosively, extending arms overhead, throwing the medicine ball off the wall, releasing the ball before the arms pass the ears, and landing in a controlled and stable manner, avoiding all compensations.
The user should avoid hyperextension of the back during the Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise.
The Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise requires the user to stand on one foot and allow their arms to extend and hang to the sides of the body with a dumbbell in each hand.

The movement phase of the Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise involves performing a biceps curl by performing elbow flexion and supination, and then slowly returning the dumbbells to their original position.
The Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise can be regressed to a two-leg variation and progressed to an alternating-arm, single-arm, or proprioceptive modality variation.
Keeping the scapulae retracted during the Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise ensures proper scapular stability, placing more emphasis on the biceps musculature.
The Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise requires the user to stand on one foot while holding a barbell in both hands.

The movement phase of the Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise involves performing a barbell curl by flexing both elbows, keeping the shoulder blades retracted, curling the bar up to chest level, and slowly lowering the bar back to the original position by extending the elbows.
The Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise can be regressed to a two-leg variation and progressed to a proprioceptive modality variation.

To decrease stress on the elbow during the Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise, the user should not grip the bar too close or too wide. The grip width should be determined by extending the elbows so the hands fall naturally to the sides, palms facing forward.
The Seated Two-Arm Dumbbell Biceps Curl exercise requires the user to sit on a bench with feet flat on the floor, pointing straight ahead, and hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms at sides.

The movement phase of the Seated Two-Arm Dumbbell Biceps Curl exercise involves performing a biceps curl by performing elbow flexion and supination, keeping the shoulder blades retracted throughout the exercise, and slowly returning the dumbbells to their original position.
The Biceps Curl Machine exercise involves flexing elbows while keeping shoulder blades retracted.
When performing biceps curls, it is important to maintain an upright posture and avoid excessive torso flexion or extension.
When performing stability ball exercises in a supine position, the head should comfortably rest on the ball to decrease stress on the cervical spine.

To perform Supine Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions, lie on a stability ball with the ball between the shoulder blades, maintaining a bridge position by contracting gluteal muscles and keeping shoulders, hips, and knees level.
Feet should be shoulder-width apart with toes pointing straight ahead during Supine Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions.
To perform Prone Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions, lie in a prone position with the stability ball under the abdomen, keeping feet pointed down, legs straight, and the navel drawn in.
Hold dumbbells in each hand with elbows bent and shoulder blades retracted and depressed during Prone Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions.
To ensure optimal alignment during Prone Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions, maintain alignment of the ankles, knees, hips, elbows, shoulders, and ears.
To perform Cable Pushdowns, stand with feet shoulder-width apart, pointed straight ahead, and knees slightly flexed.
Grasp a cable with elbows flexed and shoulder blades retracted and depressed during Cable Pushdowns.
Contract triceps by pushing hands toward the ground until arms are fully extended during Cable Pushdowns.
Using a rope when performing cable pushdowns allows the elbows to track through their natural path of motion.
To perform Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions, lie on a flat bench with feet flat and toes pointing straight ahead.
Hold the barbell in both hands with elbows flexed during Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions.
Extend elbows until arms are straight during Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions.
Slowly lower the barbell toward the forehead by flexing the elbows during Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions.
Keeping hands too close on the bar during barbell curls or triceps extensions can increase stress on the elbow.
Ball squats are a great way to teach individuals how to squat properly, with the goal to have them eventually progress to squats without the stability ball.
Stand with feet shoulders-width apart, toes pointing forward, and knees over second and third toes. Hold dumbbells to the side of the body.
Rest back against a stability ball, which is placed on a wall.
Ideally, keep feet under the knees. For individuals who lack ankle dorsiflexion (tight calves), place the feet slightly in front of the knees.
Slowly begin to squat down, bending knees and flexing hips, keeping feet straight (as if sitting into a chair). Keep the knees in line with the toes and abdominals activated.
Allow the pelvis to sit back under the ball while maintaining a neutral spine.
Keep the chest up and put pressure through the heels. Do not rely solely on the ball for support.
To rise back up, contract gluteal muscles and place pressure through the heels as knees are extended.
Stand up straight until hips and legs are fully extended. Avoid compensation in the low back or lower extremities.
Decrease range of motion
Holding on to a stable support
Squat without stability ball
Stand in front of a box, with feet pointed straight ahead. Hold dumbbells to the side.
Step onto the box with one leg, keeping foot pointed straight ahead and knee lined up over mid-foot.
Push through heel and stand up straight, balancing on one leg.
Flex the other leg at the hip and knee and dorsiflex the foot.
Return 'floating' leg to the ground and step off the box, maintaining optimal alignment.
Repeat on the other leg.
Omit balance
Decrease step height
Frontal plane step-up
Transverse plane step-up
Lunges are excellent lower-extremity strengthening exercises; however, many individuals lack the flexibility and stabilization requirements to execute the exercise properly.
Step-ups are a great way to regress the lunge until one develops proper flexibility and stabilization capabilities to perform the lunge correctly.
Stand in the machine.
Make any adjustments necessary to fit the body.
Place feet shoulders-width apart, with toes pointing forward and knees directly over second and third toes.
Slowly perform a squat to a depth that can be safely controlled. Keep optimal alignment of the lower extremities throughout the movement.
Before any compensation occurs, activate gluteal muscles and apply pressure through heels extending hips and knees back to the starting position.

Make sure the feet are positioned on the platform hips-to shoulders-width apart, toes are pointed straight, and the knees track in line with the toes. This will decrease stress to the knees, hips, and low back.
Stand with feet shoulders-width apart, toes pointing straight ahead, and knees over second and third toes.
Rest the barbell on shoulders, behind the neck, with hands grasping the bar wider than shoulders-width apart.
Slowly begin to squat down, bending knees and flexing hips, keeping feet straight. Do not allow the knees to move inward.
Keep the chest up and put pressure through the heels.
Squat to a depth that can be safely controlled while maintaining ideal posture.
To rise back up, contract gluteal muscles and place pressure through the heels as knees and hips are extended.
Stand up straight until hips and legs are fully extended. Avoid compensation in the low back or lower extremities.
Only squat as far down as can be controlled without compensating.
As one develops more flexibility and stabilization strength, the range of motion can be increased, assuming no compensations occur.
Stand with feet shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead.
Squat down slightly.
Jump up into the air while extending arms overhead.
Bring arms back to sides during landing.
Land softly on the mid-foot in a controlled manner with feet and knees pointing straight ahead. Repeat as quickly as can be controlled.
Stand with feet shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead.
Jump up off the ground and, while in the air, bring knees up to chest.
Land softly on the midpoint of the feet, in a controlled manner, with feet straight and knees over mid-foot.
Repeat, as quickly as can be controlled.

When performing power exercises, make sure you land behind the ball of the foot (not on the ball of the foot or on the heel). This will ensure proper force distribution through the foot and lower extremity, improving force production capabilities.
