Phase Two of Cardiac Rehab is prescribed by a physician if continued improvement under direct supervision is needed. It may last up to another 12 weeks and is also conducted in a cardiac rehabilitation setting.

Phases Three–Four of Cardiac Rehab are self-paced programs that involve exercise direction and motivation to sustain lifestyle changes and healthy behaviors. These phases may be conducted outside of a medical facility but require interaction between the physician and the fitness professional to ensure safety.
The principle of progressive resistance exercise shows that resistance training programs should start with light loads and increase as strength adaptations are achieved.

Two ways that the OPT model can be used in an undulating, nonlinear fashion are using weekly cycles composed of various phases of the OPT model and using weekly cycles composed of one phase of the OPT model, but with intensities and volume varied throughout the week.
To activate the correct muscles during the floor bridge, the client should move their feet to hip width and keep them straight to avoid activating the adductors.

A common hand and arm position during prone iso-abs is to bring the hands together, internally rotating at the shoulders. The arms should run parallel to each other when performing the prone iso-abs exercise.
During single-leg balance exercise, monitor feet turn out as the foot will turn out with the increased challenge of balance training in an attempt to increase the base of support.
During single-leg balance exercise, monitor anterior pelvic tilt as it is an exercise that can be incredibly demanding on the muscles around the hips and spine.
During single-leg stance, the gluteal muscles need to work with the abdominal muscles to keep the hips and the stance leg in proper position.

During the staggered stance, it is very common for the rear leg to turn out, exacerbating the compensation of the feet turning out. The rear leg should be straight, and if necessary, the client can get the rear leg into a triple-extended position.
If the hip flexors are restricting motion, then the front of the hips will likely be pulled down, exacerbating the compensation.

Performing many repetitions of lat pull-downs, pull-ups, or heavy chest exercises may make the compensation worse. If the client moves into an anterior pelvic tilt, overhead movements should be avoided until proper extensibility has been restored to the lats and pectorals.
Try to avoid the negative connotation that often comes with the word ‘homework.’ It is important to get clients to realize that repetition enables them to progress more quickly.

Many clients pursuing a weight loss goal suffer from a lack of motivation or even anxiety toward exercise. Some will be motivated to go to the gym and perform an hour-long workout session without the trainer, whereas others may prefer to perform flexibility and light cardio, such as a walk in the neighborhood.
To encourage completion, training should begin with simple tasks, and fitness professionals should periodically assess the client’s readiness to take on more complex exercises.
A training template should track the phase of training, exercise selection, intensity, volume, and outcome.
Muscle imbalances can in large part be attributed to the client’s lifestyle and daily activity pattern.
There is no amount of foam-rolling, stretching, or strengthening that can correct poor daily habits.
Fitness professionals should teach their clients how to move when performing basic tasks throughout their day.
The client learning how to move properly when not in the gym may be the most important factor when considering muscle imbalances.
This will require the fitness professional to provide the client with movement-specific homework.
The supine bridge engages the gluteal muscles, which should be very powerful muscles.
The prone iso-abs teaches the muscles that are designed to stabilize the spine to engage.
The scapular exercise uses the entire shoulder girdle in the most functional plane of motion.
This is the recommended beginning exercise for most clients once the client is able to coordinate the muscular activity.
Can be used with any movement compensation as long as the client can safely perform it.
Rowing motions will teach scapular retraction and depression as well as strengthen muscles that are often weak.
The squat uses two of the largest muscle groups in the body, the back and the gluteals, as well as all of the muscles around the core.
The squat requires work from many very large muscles, and adequate range of motion in some very important joints.
The lunge is similar to a squat with a different footprint.
The deadlift is important for clients who lack the range of motion to squat but need to work their gluteal muscles.
The feet typically turn out during calf raises because the calves are shortened and overactive.
The knees cave in during adductor machine exercises because the adductors are already overactive.
The abductor machine may appear to be indicated with knees caving in.
Leg extension machines focus on the quadriceps muscle group, so isolating the quads with that movement compensation could potentially make it worse.
The leg press is a quad-dominant machine and does not allow the gluteal muscles to work to their full potential.
Some of the adductors also serve as hip flexors (pectineus and adductor brevis). Isolating the adductors will potentially make the compensation worse.
Leg raises strengthen the hip flexors, which are overactive in an anterior pelvic tilt.
Leg extension is designed to isolate the quadriceps, so isolating the quadriceps is not recommended in the presence of an anterior pelvic tilt.

Leg curl may be performed incorrectly due to inflexibility of the quadriceps and hip flexors, weakness of the hamstrings, and overactivity of the low back muscles, all indicated by anterior pelvic tilt.
Muscles that control the scapula in upward rotation should be worked on before performing lat strengthening work.
The chest press machine is often used with a significant amount of weight, which increases the strength of the prime movers without teaching proper stabilization.
The shoulder press is not harmful, but if the lats are inflexible then clients will likely arch their low back in order to successfully execute the shoulder press.
Women should consume 2.7 L (91 oz.) of water per day.
Men should consume 3.7 L (125 oz.) of water per day.
2 hours pre-exercise, consume 14–20 oz. of water.
15 minutes pre-exercise, consume 16 oz. of water, if tolerated.
During exercise, consume 4–8 oz. of water every 15–20 minutes or 16–32 ounces every hour.
Postexercise, consume 50 ounces for every kilogram (2.2 pounds) of body weight lost.
Food provides 600–800 mL of water intake.
Beverages provide 1,000 mL of water intake.
Metabolic water (from digestion) provides 200–300 mL of water intake.
Urine provides 900–1,200 mL of water output.
Mild sweating provides 400 mL of water output.
Lungs provide 300 mL of water output.
Feces provide 200 mL of water output.

Dry mouth, headache, rapid heartbeat, sleepiness or tiredness, constipation, rapid breathing, thirst, dizziness, fever, decreased urine output, sunken eyes, delirium, dry skin, low blood pressure, and unconsciousness are signs of dehydration.
Mobile applications can provide guidance to clients while they are traveling or even through remote personal training.

Mobile applications can organize and track exercise order (with visual demonstrations of exercise technique), sets, reps, and even rest periods can be prescribed through an app, allowing the client to complete workouts on their own while on vacation, traveling for business, or away from the gym.
Professionals can use mobile applications to monitor workload, whether the workout is performed in person or remote.
Mobile applications can facilitate encouragement and motivation from the professional and surrounding community by using social networks.
Nutritional apps can provide valuable information and assistance on client’s habits throughout the day/week.
Ensure high fluid intake for several days before competition (urine should be pale in color).
Consume 14–20 ounces (1.75–2.5 cups) of fluid 2 hours before exercise.
Consume 16 ounces about 15 minutes before exercise, if tolerated.
Consume water or sports drinks rather than soda or juice.
Fluid absorption is accelerated with a 6% carbohydrate drink (any popular sports drink).
Cold water or fluid is more rapidly absorbed.
Drink 4–8 ounces (0.5–1.0 cup) every 15–20 minutes or 16–32 ounces of fluid every hour during exercise.
If the weather is very hot, more fluid may be required during exercise.
Consuming fluids with 500–700 grams of sodium per 33 ounces of water enhances fluid replacement during exercise.
Drink sports drinks containing 6–8% glucose for exercise lasting longer than 60 minutes.
Sodas, teas, and juices are not ideal, and may result in the reverse of the desired effect during exercise.
Drinking plain water without electrolytes can also be a problem during exercise.
Take electrolyte pills with water and/or eat food if sports drinks are not favorable during exercise.
Consume 50 ounces of fluid for every kilogram (2.2 pounds) of body weight lost after exercise.
For exercise longer than 1 hour in duration, consuming a drink containing sodium and glucose will promote rapid rehydration after exercise.
Activity trackers can track movement and provide information regarding physical activity patterns.
By monitoring physical activity throughout the day among their clients, fitness professionals can help guide people toward greater activity and facilitate faster results.
Some activity trackers allow for the monitoring of heart rate, which can be valuable data for a fitness professional.
Lower heart rate during work and rest signifies a more efficient and fit cardiorespiratory system.
Sleep patterns can be tracked by some activity trackers, enabling for the evaluation of quality of sleep along with alterations in training plans as needed.
Fitness professionals should explain to clients how various mobile apps will be utilized and why their use is beneficial.
Fitness professionals should consider the different audiences that each social media outlet can help them access and utilize each in strategic ways.
Each social media outlet is a powerful method of inexpensive promotion of personal training services and offers a unique opportunity for the fitness professional to position himself/herself.
Social media is instrumental in helping develop relationships between professionals and their clients.

The primary goals of utilizing social media to further personal training business are: Connect with current and potential clients outside of the gym, Establish a position of expertise and leadership, Share information about services, events, and training opportunities.

Additional goals of utilizing social media to further personal training business are: Encourage and motivate clients, Provide educational messages, Show relevancy in the industry, Establish credibility.

Examples of educational posts that can help the professional gain credibility include: Short videos discussing exercise techniques for various modalities, Blog posts discussing a recent research article, Suggestions for creative ways of overcoming barriers to fitness, Recommendations of training strategies for specific populations such as weight loss or older adults, Examples of training plans for a variety of clients, Philosophical statements that describe the fitness professional’s approach to program design along with examples of how programs are altered based on individual needs, Fitness tip of the day.
Determining what fitness trends are simply fads and what technologies will develop and thrive is helpful to planning ahead in the fitness industry.
It may be difficult to really know what consumers will gravitate towards in coming years.
Wearable technology is an area that will continue to see the greatest interest and development in fitness technology.

Devices that can be synced to smart phones to track and display exercise habits in real time may be an area of rapid growth as well as the development of new apps and greater physiological variables being monitored by each device.
The development of fitness apps is projected to continue, especially now that app developers are becoming more accessible to fitness professionals.
Self-confidence can lead to greater exercise adherence, which, in turn, results in more confidence, which leads to increased exercise.
Fitness professionals should try to build their clients’ confidence so they can initiate and maintain a commitment to exercise.
Performance accomplishments are the strongest source of self-confidence.
Performance accomplishments focus on personal task improvement and success, rather than on comparisons with others.
Watching other, similar individuals successfully perform the desired task can increase a person’s confidence in his or her ability to complete the task.
Being persuaded by someone else (e.g., fitness professional, coach, friend) that he or she can perform the task successfully can increase confidence.
When people imagine themselves performing a task, it increases their confidence that they will actually be able to perform it.
Motivational interviewing has been defined as a collaborative person-centered form of guiding to elicit and strengthen motivation for change.
The foundation of motivational interviewing is an empathetic, person-centered style, emphasizing evoking and strengthening the client’s own motivation for healthy change.

Motivational interviewing involves knowledge and skills in four areas: expressing empathy, helping the client realize the gap between values and problematic behavior (developing discrepancy), respecting the client’s resistance as being normal, and supporting the client’s self-efficacy.
It is the client’s task, not the fitness professional’s, to articulate the client’s ambivalence (exercise vs. not exercising).
Motivation to change is elicited from the client.
Readiness to change is not a client trait, but rather a fluctuating product of interpersonal interaction (the fitness professional might be assuming a greater readiness for change than is the case).
The autonomy-supportive style of coaching focuses on creating an environment that emphasizes self-improvement, rather than beating others (i.e., direct competition).

Fitness professionals can support their clients’ autonomy by providing choices within limits, offering rationales for activity structures, recognizing clients’ feelings and perspectives, creating opportunities for clients to demonstrate initiative, avoiding overt control and criticism, providing informational feedback, and limiting clients’ ego involvement throughout their program (i.e., focus on self-improvement instead of comparing to others).
Prompting is a cue that initiates a behavior.
Prompts can be verbal, physical, or symbolic.
A verbal prompt can be a slogan or phrase that encourages the client.
A physical prompt might be helping someone get over a ‘sticking point’ in an activity.
A symbolic prompt generally reminds a person to begin or continue a behavior, such as leaving out one’s workout gear the night before to promote physical activity.
The goal is to increase cues for the desired behavior and decrease cues for competing behaviors.
Written statements that outline specific behaviors and establish consequences for fulfillment (or lack thereof) are known as contracts.
Contracts typically specify expectations, responsibilities, and contingencies for behavioral change.
The purpose of contracts is to help the client take action, establishing criteria for meeting goals, and providing a means for clarifying consequences.
Contracts increase the individual’s public commitment and foster a sense of self-control.
A performance or attendance graph usually represents data in a form that is easily understood by everyone involved.
The visual representation of progress by a chart is extremely helpful because a client can note even small changes in behavior and performance.
This public information allows fitness professionals and the clients to offer praise and encouragement for the client on the chart.
Capitalizing on individuals’ inherent interest to reach certain outcomes provides periodic, positive feedback on the progress that has been made.
Feedback is provided with specific information on performing the behavior in question.
This approach focuses on ways to help someone solve current issues.
The focus will rely upon addressing thoughts and behaviors that are detrimental to the growth of the individual.
Association occurs when the focus is on internal body feedback (e.g., how their muscles feel or their breathing feels).
Dissociation occurs when the focus is on the external environment (e.g., noticing how pretty the scenery is or listening to music while exercising).
Social support refers to an individual’s favorable attitude toward another person’s involvement in an exercise program.
This approach emphasizes the internal enjoyment and fun of exercise, and making it something to look forward to—not just as a means to an external goal such as weight loss (Kimiecik, 2002).
Professionals should help clients find activities they enjoy.
Keeping accurate records is an important part of a fitness professional’s job both for business purposes and for designing and implementing workouts.
Details matter in running a successful long-lasting business.
Fitness professionals often see several clients in a row, which makes it all the more important to write down an outline for the workout in each person’s file.
Having a chart/folder to refer to, and making notes during or soon after the session, makes for better and more efficient fitness professionals.
It is important that fitness professionals record not only clients’ objective workout results, but also what was discussed during the workout.
Fitness professionals should keep two types of records.
The first type is concerned with clients’ progress.
Clients come in with different abilities, needs, and goals; these should be individualized and tracked on a daily, weekly, or monthly basis, depending on what is most germane to the client.
The other type of records that professionals should keep is concerned with the business aspect of the job.
The specific items recorded will vary based on the job responsibilities, but the following are some typical records that are kept on a daily, weekly, monthly, or yearly basis:
Number of days worked
Average income (per hour)
Number of days off
Client hours cancelled/rescheduled
Percentage of cancellations that were paid
Mileage driven (to in-home clients)
Average number of sessions
Average time spent in the gym not getting paid
A multitude of tactics can be used to study the content you have learned in your CPT course to prepare for the credentialing exam.
Use the study approach that maximizes your ability to retain and recall the information you learned.
However, regardless of the study approach, it is important to prepare for the CPT exam in a kinesthetic way.
This will allow you to have the same memory recall while taking the CPT exam.
Performing an isolated contraction would give you firsthand experience on how a problematic muscle feels.
So, if you are stuck on a question that tests the concept of a problematic muscle, you may practice the movement of the muscle to refresh your memory.
Don’t try to learn everything the night before the exam. Structure your schedule to allow for ample time to study and review the CPT material.
Get 8 hours of sleep before the exam. It is essential to get enough rest to be able to focus during the exam.
Eat before the exam. Ensuring you have sufficient energy to sit through the exam is vital for your ability to focus.
Focus on the test in front of you.
If you don’t know an answer, mark it and move on. Spending time on a question you don’t know will do little good.
Don’t worry if others finish before you. Finishing first or last has nothing to do with your score. Make good use of the time allotted to you.
All questions are multiple choice, providing you four answer choices with 4 choices.
The CPT exam questions are focused on the objective criteria rather than the subjective criteria.
The A-Band is the region of the sarcomere where myosin filaments are predominantly seen with minor overlap of the actin filaments.
Abduction is a movement in the frontal plane away from the midline of the body.
Acceleration is when a muscle exerts more force than is being placed on it, the muscle will shorten; also known as a concentric contraction or force production.
Acidosis is the accumulation of excessive hydrogen that causes increased acidity of the blood and muscle.
Actin is one of the two major myofilaments, actin is the ‘thin’ filament that acts along with myosin to produce muscular contraction.
An action potential is a nerve impulse that allows neurons to transmit information.
Active flexibility is the ability of agonists and synergists to move a limb through the full range of motion while their functional antagonist is being stretched.
Active-isolated stretch is the process of using agonists and synergists to dynamically move the joint into a range of motion.
Acute variables are important components that specify how each exercise is to be performed.
Adaptive means capable of changing for a specific use.
Adduction is movement in the frontal plane back toward the midline of the body.
Adenosine diphosphate (ADP) is a high-energy compound occurring in all cells from which adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is formed.
Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is an energy storage and transfer unit within the cells of the body.

Adequate intake (AI) is a recommended average daily nutrient intake level, based on observed (or experimentally determined) approximations or estimates of nutrient intake that are assumed to be adequate for a group (or groups) of healthy people; this measure is used when an RDA cannot be determined.
Advanced stage is the second stage of the dynamic pattern perspective theory when learners gain the ability to alter and manipulate the movements more efficiently to adapt to environmental changes.
Aerobic means activities requiring oxygen.
Afferent neurons (also known as sensory neurons) gather incoming sensory information from the environment and deliver it to the central nervous system.
Agility is the ability to accelerate, decelerate, stabilize, and change direction quickly while maintaining proper posture.
An agonist is a muscle that is the primary mover in a joint motion; also known as a prime mover.
The alarm reaction is the first stage of the General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), the initial reaction to a stressor.
Altered reciprocal inhibition is the concept of muscle inhibition, caused by a tight agonist, which inhibits its functional antagonist.
The amortization phase is the electromechanical delay a muscle experiences in the transition from eccentric to concentric muscle action.
Anaerobic activities do not require oxygen.

The anaerobic threshold is the point during high-intensity activity when the body can no longer meet its demand for oxygen and anaerobic metabolism predominates; it is also called the lactate threshold.
Anatomic locations refers to terms that describe locations on the body.
The anatomic position is the position with the body erect with the arms at the sides and the palms forward.
The anatomic position is the position of reference for anatomic nomenclature.
Anatomic terms such as anterior and posterior, medial and lateral, and abduction and adduction apply to the body when it is in the anatomic position.
An annual plan is a generalized training plan that spans 1 year to show when the client will progress between phases.
Antagonists are muscles that act in direct opposition to agonists (prime movers).
Anterior (or ventral) means on the front of the body.
The aortic semilunar valve controls blood flow from the left ventricle to the aorta going to the entire body.
The appendicular skeleton is the portion of the skeletal system that includes the upper and lower extremities.
Arteries are vessels that transport blood away from the heart.
Arterioles are small terminal branches of an artery, which end in capillaries.
Arteriosclerosis is a general term that refers to hardening (and loss of elasticity) of arteries.
Arthritis is chronic inflammation of the joints.
Arthrokinematics is 1. joint motion. 2. the motions of joints in the body.

Arthrokinetic dysfunction is 1. a biomechanical and neuromuscular dysfunction in which forces at the joint are altered, resulting in abnormal joint movement and proprioception. 2. altered forces at the joint that result in abnormal muscular activity and impaired neuromuscular communication at the joint.
Arthrokinetic inhibition is the neuromuscular phenomenon that occurs when a joint dysfunction inhibits the muscles that surround the joint.
Articular (hyaline) cartilage is cartilage that covers the articular surfaces of bones.
Articulation is junctions of bones, muscles, and connective tissue at which movement occurs; it is also known as a joint.
Assessment is a process of determining the importance, size, or value of something.
The association stage is Fitts’ second stage in which learners become more consistent with their movement with practice.

Atherosclerosis is 1. clogging, narrowing, and hardening of the body’s large arteries and medium-sized blood vessels. Atherosclerosis can lead to stroke, heart attack, eye problems, and kidney problems. 2. buildup of fatty plaques in arteries that leads to narrowing and reduced blood flow.
Atmospheric pressure is everyday pressure in the air.

The atrioventricular (AV) node is a small mass of specialized cardiac muscle fibers, located in the wall of the right atrium of the heart, that receives heartbeat impulses from the sinoatrial node and directs them to the walls of the ventricles.
Atrioventricular Valves allow for proper blood flow from the atria to the ventricles.
The atrium is the superior chamber of the heart that receives blood from the veins and forces it into the ventricles.
Augmented feedback is information provided by some external source such as a fitness professional, videotape, or a heart rate monitor.
Autogenic inhibition is the process by which neural impulses that sense tension are greater than the impulses that cause muscles to contract, providing an inhibitory effect to the muscle spindles.
The autonomous stage is Fitt’s third stage of motor learning in which the learner has refined the skill to a level of automation.
The axial skeleton is the portion of the skeletal system that consists of the skull, rib cage, and vertebral column.
An axon is a cylindric projection from the cell body that transmits nervous impulses to other neurons or effector sites.
Backside mechanics is proper alignment of the rear leg and pelvis during sprinting, which includes ankle plantar flexion, knee extension, hip extension, and neutral pelvis.
Balance is the ability to sustain or return the body’s center of mass or line of gravity over its base of support.
Balance is also when the body is in equilibrium and stationary, meaning no linear or angular movement.
Ball-and-socket joints are the most-mobile joints that allow motion in all three planes. Examples include the shoulder and hip.
The basal ganglia is a portion of the lower brain that is instrumental in the initiation and control of repetitive voluntary movements such as walking and running.

Beta-oxidation (α-oxidation) is the breakdown of triglycerides into smaller subunits called free fatty acids (FFAs) to convert FFAs into acyl-CoA molecules, which then are available to enter the Krebs cycle and ultimately lead to the production of additional ATP.
The bicuspid (mitral) valve has two cusps that control the blood flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle.
The bioenergetic continuum is the three main pathways used by the kinetic chain to produce ATP.
Bioenergetics is the study of energy in the human body.
Biomechanics is a study that uses principles of physics to quantitatively study how forces interact within a living body.
Biomechanics is also the science concerned with the internal and external forces acting on the human body and the effects produced by these forces.
Bipenniform muscle fibers are muscle fibers that are arranged with short, oblique fibers that extend from both sides of a long tendon. An example is the rectus femoris.
Blood is fluid that circulates in the heart, arteries, capillaries, and veins, carries nutrients and oxygen to all parts of the body, and also rids the body of waste products.
Blood lipids, also known as cholesterol and triglycerides, are carried in the bloodstream by protein molecules known as high-density lipoproteins (HDL) and low-density lipoproteins (LDL).
Blood vessels are a network of hollow tubes that circulate blood throughout the body.
Bones provide a resting ground for muscles and protection of vital organs.
Bracing occurs when you have contracted both the abdominal, lower back, and buttock muscles at the same time.
The brainstem is the link between the sensory and motor nerves coming from the brain to the body and vice versa.
A calorie is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of 1 g of water 1°C.
A Calorie is a unit of expression of energy equal to 1000 cal. It is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a kilogram or liter of water 1°C.

Cancer is any of various types of malignant neoplasms, most of which invade surrounding tissues, may metastasize to several sites, and are likely to recur after attempted removal and to cause death of the patient unless adequately treated.
Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels, and the site of exchange of chemicals and water between the blood and the tissues.

Carbohydrates are organic compounds of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, which include starches, cellulose, and sugars, and are an important source of energy. All carbohydrates are eventually broken down in the body to glucose, a simple sugar.
Carbohydrates are neutral compounds of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen (such as sugars, starches, and celluloses), which make up a large portion of animal foods.
Cardiac muscle is heart muscle.
Cardiac output (Q) is heart rate multiplied by stroke volume, the overall performance of the heart.
Cardiorespiratory fitness is the ability of the circulatory and respiratory systems to supply oxygen-rich blood to skeletal muscles during sustained physical activity.
The cardiorespiratory system is a system of the body composed of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems.
Cardiorespiratory training is any physical activity that involves and places stress on the cardiorespiratory system.
The cardiovascular control center (CVC) directs impulses that will either increase or decrease cardiac output and peripheral resistance based on feedback from all structures involved.
The cardiovascular system is a system of the body composed of the heart, blood, and blood vessels.
The cell body is the portion of the neuron that contains the nucleus, lysosomes, mitochondria, and a Golgi complex.
The central controller controls heart rate, left ventricular contractility, and arterial blood pressure by manipulating the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems.
The central nervous system is the portion of the nervous system that consists of the brain and spinal cord.

The cerebellum is a portion of the lower brain that compares sensory information from the body and the external environment with motor information from the cerebral cortex to ensure smooth coordinated movement.
The cerebral cortex is a portion of the central nervous system that consists of the frontal lobe, parietal lobe, occipital lobe, and temporal lobe.
The cervical spine is the area of the spine containing the seven vertebrae that compose the neck.
A chain is a system that is linked together or connected.
Chemoreceptors are sensory receptors that respond to chemical interaction (smell and taste).
Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease is the condition of altered airflow through the lungs, generally caused by airway obstruction as a result of mucus production.
Circuit Training System consists of a series of exercises that an individual performs one after another with minimal rest.
Co-contraction is when muscles contract together in a force-couple.
The Cognitive Stage is Fitts's first stage of motor learning that describes how the learner spends much of the time thinking about what they are about to perform.
Collagen is a protein that is found in connective tissue that provides tensile strength. Collagen, unlike elastin, is not very elastic.
Compound-Sets involve the performance of two exercises for antagonistic muscles. For example, a set of bench presses followed by cable rows (chest/back).
Concentric Muscle Action is when a muscle is exerting force greater than the resistive force, resulting in shortening of the muscle.
Conduction Passageway consists of all the structures that air travels through before entering the respiratory passageway.
Condyles are projections protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach; also known as a process, epicondyle, tubercle, and trochanter.
A Condyloid Joint is a joint where the condyle of one bone fits into the elliptical cavity of another bone to form the joint. An example would include the knee joint.
Contralateral means positioned on the opposite side of the body.
Controlled Instability is a training environment that is as unstable as can safely be controlled by an individual.

Core refers to 1. the center of the body and the beginning point for movement. 2. the structures that make up the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex (LPHC), including the lumbar spine, the pelvic girdle, abdomen, and the hip joint.
Core Strength is the ability of the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex musculature to control an individual’s constantly changing center of gravity.
The Coronal Plane is an imaginary plane that bisects the body to create front and back halves; also known as the frontal plane.
Corrective Flexibility is designed to improve muscle imbalances and altered arthrokinematics.
Creatine Phosphate is a high-energy phosphate molecule that is stored in cells and can be used to resynthesize ATP immediately.
The Cumulative Injury Cycle is a cycle whereby an injury will induce inflammation, muscle spasm, adhesions, altered neuromuscular control, and muscle imbalances.
Davis’s Law states that soft tissue models along the line of stress.
Decelerate is when the muscle is exerting less force than is being placed on it, the muscle lengthens; also known as an eccentric muscle action or force reduction.
Deconditioned is a state of lost physical fitness, which may include muscle imbalances, decreased flexibility, and a lack of core and joint stability.
Dendrites are a portion of the neuron responsible for gathering information from other structures.
Depressions are flattened or indented portions of bone, which can be muscle attachment sites.
Delayed-Onset Muscle Soreness is pain or discomfort often felt 24 to 72 hours after intense exercise or unaccustomed physical activity.
Diabetes Mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder caused by insulin deficiency, which impairs carbohydrate usage and enhances usage of fats and proteins.
The diaphysis is the shaft portion of a long bone.
A dietary supplement is a substance that completes or makes an addition to daily dietary intake.
Diffusion is the process of getting oxygen from the environment to the tissues of the body.
Distal refers to a position farthest from the center of the body, or point of reference.
Dorsal refers to a position on the back or toward the back of the body.
Dorsiflexion, when applied to the ankle, is the ability to bend at the ankle, moving the front of the foot upward.
Dynamic balance is the ability to move and change directions under various conditions without falling.
The drawing-in maneuver is the activation of the transverse abdominis, multifidus, pelvic floor muscles, and diaphragm to provide core stabilization.
Dynamic functional flexibility is multiplanar soft tissue extensibility with optimal neuromuscular efficiency throughout the full range of motion.
Dynamic joint stabilization is the ability of the stabilizing muscles of a joint to produce optimum stabilization during functional, multiplanar movements.

The Dynamic Pattern Perspective (DPP) is the theory that suggests that movement patterns are produced as a result of the combined interactions among many systems (nervous, muscular, skeletal, mechanical, environmental, past experiences, and so forth).
Dynamic range of motion is the combination of flexibility and the nervous system’s ability to control this range of motion efficiently.
Dynamic stabilization occurs when a muscle is exerting force equal to the force being placed on it. It is also known as an isometric contraction.
Dynamic stretching uses the force production of a muscle and the body’s momentum to take a joint through the full available range of motion.
An eccentric muscle action occurs when a muscle develops tension while lengthening.
Effectors are any structure innervated by the nervous system including organs, glands, muscle tissue, connective tissue, blood vessels, bone marrow, and so forth.
Efferent neurons are neurons that transmit nerve impulses from the brain or spinal cord to the effector sites such as muscles or glands; also known as motor neurons.
Elastin is a protein found in connective tissue that has elastic properties.
Empathy is the action of awareness, understanding, and sensitivity of the thoughts, emotions, and experience of another without personally having gone through the same.
The endocrine system is the system of glands in the human body that is responsible for producing hormones.
The endomysium is the deepest layer of connective tissue that surrounds individual muscle fibers.
Endurance strength is the ability to produce and maintain force for prolonged periods.
Energy is the capacity to do work.
Energy-utilizing refers to when energy is gathered from an energy-yielding source by some storage unit (ATP) and then transferred to a site that can use this energy.
Enjoyment is the amount of pleasure derived from performing a physical activity.
An epicondyle is a projection protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach; also known as a condyle, process, tubercle, and trochanter.
The epimysium is a layer of connective tissue that is underneath the fascia and surrounds the muscle.
The epiphyseal plates are the region of long bone connecting the diaphysis to the epiphysis. It is a layer of subdividing cartilaginous cells in which growth in length of the diaphysis occurs.

The epiphysis is the end of long bones, which is mainly composed of cancellous bone, and house much of the red marrow involved in red blood cell production. They are also one of the primary sites for bone growth.
Equilibrium is a condition of balance between opposed forces, influences, or actions.
Erythrocytes are red blood cells.

The Estimated Average Requirement (EAR) is the average daily nutrient intake level that is estimated to meet the requirement of half the healthy individuals who are in a particular life stage and gender group.
Eversion is a movement in which the inferior calcaneus moves laterally.
Excess Postexercise Oxygen Consumption (EPOC) is the state in which the body’s metabolism is elevated after exercise.
Excitation-Contraction Coupling is the process of neural stimulation creating a muscle contraction.
Exercise Imagery is the process created to produce internalized experiences to support or enhance exercise participation.
Exercise Metabolism is the examination of bioenergetics as it relates to the unique physiologic changes and demands placed on the body during exercise.
Exercise Order refers to the order in which the exercises are performed during a workout.
Exercise Selection is the process of choosing appropriate exercises for a client’s program.
Exhaustion is the third stage of the General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), when prolonged stress or stress that is intolerable produces exhaustion or distress to the system.
The Expert Stage is the third stage of the dynamic pattern perspective model in which the learner now focuses on recognizing and coordinating their joint motions in the most efficient manner.
Expiration is the process of actively or passively relaxing the inspiratory muscles to move air out of the body.
Explosive Strength is the ability to develop a sharp rise in force production once a movement pattern has been initiated.
Extensibility is the capability to be elongated or stretched.
Extension is a straightening movement in which the relative angle between two adjacent segments increases.
External Feedback is information provided by some external source, such as a health and fitness professional, videotape, mirror, or heart rate monitor, to supplement the internal environment.
External Rotation is the rotation of a joint away from the middle of the body.

A Fan-Shaped Muscle is a muscular fiber arrangement that has muscle fibers span out from a narrow attachment at one end to a broad attachment at the other end. An example would be the pectoralis major.
Fascia is the outermost layer of connective tissue that surrounds the muscle.
A Fascicle is a grouping of muscle fibers that house myofibrils.

Fast Twitch Fibers are muscle fibers that can also be characterized by the term type IIA and IIB. These fibers contain fewer capillaries, mitochondria, and myoglobin. These fibers fatigue faster than type I fibers.

Fat is one of the three main classes of foods and a source of energy in the body. Fats help the body use some vitamins and keep the skin healthy. They also serve as energy stores for the body. In food, there are two types of fats, saturated and unsaturated.

Feedback is 1. The utilization of sensory information and sensorimotor integration to aid the kinetic chain in the development of permanent neural representations of motor patterns. 2. The use of sensory information and sensorimotor integration to help the human movement system in motor learning.
Flat Bones are a classification of bone that is involved in protection and provides attachment sites for muscles. Examples include the sternum and scapulae.
Flexibility is the normal extensibility of all soft tissues that allows the full range of motion of a joint.
Flexibility Training is physical training of the body that integrates various stretches in all three planes of motion to produce the maximum extensibility of tissues.
Flexion is a bending movement in which the relative angle between two adjacent segments decreases.
Force is an influence applied by one object to another, which results in an acceleration or deceleration of the second object.
A Force-Couple is muscle groups moving together to produce movement around a joint.
The Force-Velocity Curve is the ability of muscles to produce force with increasing velocity.
Formed Elements refers to the cellular component of blood that includes erythrocytes, leukocytes, and thrombocytes.
A Fossa is a depression or indented portion of bone, which could be a muscle attachment site; also known as a depression.
Frequency is the number of training sessions in a given timeframe.
The frontal lobe is a portion of the cerebral cortex that contains structures necessary for the planning and control of voluntary movement.
The frontal plane is an imaginary bisector that divides the body into front and back halves.
Frontside mechanics is proper alignment of the lead leg and pelvis during sprinting, which includes ankle dorsiflexion, knee flexion, hip flexion, and neutral pelvis.
Fructose is known as fruit sugar; it is a member of the simple sugars carbohydrate group found in fruits, honey, and syrups, and certain vegetables.
Functional efficiency is the ability of the nervous and muscular systems to move in the most efficient manner while placing the least amount of stress on the kinetic chain.
Functional flexibility is integrated, multiplanar, soft tissue extensibility with optimum neuromuscular control through the full range of motion.
Functional strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to perform dynamic eccentric, isometric, and concentric contractions efficiently in a multiplanar environment.
A fusiform muscular fiber arrangement has a full muscle belly that tapers off at both ends. An example would include the biceps brachii.
General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) is a syndrome in which the kinetic chain responds and adapts to imposed demands.
General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) is a term used to describe how the body responds and adapts to stress.
A generalized motor program is a motor program for a distinct category of movements or actions, such as overhand throwing, kicking, or running.
A general warm-up consists of movements that do not necessarily have any movement specificity to the actual activity to be performed.
A general warm-up is low-intensity exercise consisting of movements that do not necessarily relate to the more intense exercise that is to follow.
A gliding joint is a nonaxial joint that moves back and forth or side to side. Examples would include the carpals of the hand and the facet joints.
Gluconeogenesis is the formation of glucose from noncarbohydrate sources, such as amino acids.
Glucose is a simple sugar manufactured by the body from carbohydrates, fat, and to a lesser extent protein, which serves as the body’s main source of fuel.
The glycemic index is a ranking of carbohydrate-containing foods based on the food’s effect on blood sugar compared with a standard reference food’s effect.

Glycogen is the complex carbohydrate molecule used to store carbohydrates in the liver and muscle cells. When carbohydrate energy is needed, glycogen is converted into glucose for use by the muscle cells.
Golgi tendon organs are receptors sensitive to change in tension of the muscle and the rate of that change.
Goniometric assessment is a technique measuring angular measurement and joint range of motion.
Gravity is the attraction between earth and the objects on earth.
Ground reaction force (GRF) is the equal and opposite force that is exerted back onto the body with every step that is taken.
The heart is a hollow muscular organ that pumps a circulation of blood through the body by means of rhythmic contraction.
Heart rate (HR) is the rate at which the heart pumps.
Hemoglobin is oxygen-carrying component of red blood cells and also gives blood its red color.
Hierarchical theories are theories that propose all planning and implementation of movement result from one or more higher brain centers.
A hinge joint is a uniaxial joint that allows movement in one plane of motion. Examples would include the elbow and ankle.
Hobbies are activities that a client may partake in regularly, but which may not necessarily be athletic in nature.
Homeostasis is the ability or tendency of an organism or a cell to maintain internal equilibrium by adjusting its physiologic processes.
Horizontal abduction is movement of the arm or thigh in the transverse plane from an anterior position to a lateral position.
Horizontal adduction is movement of the arm or thigh in the transverse plane from a lateral position to an anterior position.
Horizontal loading is performing all sets of an exercise or body part before moving on to the next exercise or body part.
Human movement science is the study of functional anatomy, functional biomechanics, motion learning, and motor control.
The human movement system is the combination and interrelation of the nervous, muscular, and skeletal systems.
Hypercholesterolemia is chronic high levels of cholesterol in the bloodstream.
Hyperglycemia is abnormally high blood sugar.
Hyperlipidemia is elevated levels of blood fats (e.g., triglycerides, cholesterol).
Hyperextension is extension of a joint beyond the normal limit or range of motion.
Hypertension is consistently elevated arterial blood pressure, which, if sustained at a high enough level, is likely to induce cardiovascular or end-organ damage.
Hypertrophy is enlargement of skeletal muscle fibers in response to overcoming force from high volumes of tension.
The H-zone is the area of the sarcomere where only myosin filaments are present.
The I-band is the area of the sarcomere where only actin filaments are present.
Inferior is positioned below a point of reference.
Insertion is the part of a muscle by which it is attached to the part to be moved—compare with origin.
Inspiration is the process of actively contracting the inspiratory muscles to move air into the body.
Insulin is a protein hormone released by the pancreas that helps glucose move out of the blood and into the cells in the body, where the glucose can be used as energy and nourishment.

Integrated Cardiorespiratory Training programs systematically progress clients through various stages to achieve optimal levels of physiologic, physical, and performance adaptations by placing stress on the cardio-respiratory system.

A systematic problem-solving method that provides the fitness professional with a basis for making educated decisions about exercise and acute variable selection is called the Integrated Fitness Profile.
Integrated Flexibility Training is a multifaceted approach integrating various flexibility techniques to achieve optimum soft tissue extensibility in all planes of motion.
To move with efficiency, forces must be dampened (eccentrically), stabilized (isometrically), and then accelerated (concentrically) according to the Integrated Performance Paradigm.

Integrated Training applies all forms of training such as integrated flexibility training, integrated cardiorespiratory training, neuromuscular stabilization (balance), core stabilization, and reactive neuromuscular training (power), and integrated strength training.

The ability of the nervous system to analyze and interpret sensory information to allow for proper decision making, which produces the appropriate response, is called Integrative (Function of Nervous System).
The level of demand that a given activity places on the body is called Intensity.
Intermittent Claudication is the manifestation of the symptoms caused by peripheral arterial disease.
The process whereby sensory information is used by the body to reactively monitor movement and the environment is called Internal Feedback.
Internal Rotation is rotation of a joint toward the middle of the body.
Interneurons transmit nerve impulses from one neuron to another.
The ability of the neuromuscular system to allow all muscles to work together with proper activation and timing between them is called Intermuscular Coordination.
The ability of the neuromuscular system to allow optimal levels of motor unit recruitment and synchronization within a muscle is called Intramuscular Coordination.
Pressure within the thoracic cavity is called Intrapulmonary Pressure.
Inversion is a movement in which the inferior calcaneus moves medially.
Ipsilateral means positioned on the same side of the body.

A classification of bone that has its own unique shape and function, which does not fit the characteristics of the other categories is called Irregular Bones. Examples include the vertebrae and pelvic bones.
Isokinetic Muscle Action occurs when a muscle shortens at a constant speed over the full range of motion.
Isometric Muscle Action occurs when a muscle is exerting force equal to the force being placed on it leading to no visible change in the muscle length.
Junctions of bones, muscles, and connective tissue at which movement occurs; also known as an articulation, are called Joints.
Movement in a plane occurs about an axis running perpendicular to the plane.
Receptors surrounding a joint that respond to pressure, acceleration, and deceleration of the joint are called Joint Receptors.
Resistance to unwanted movement is called Joint Stiffness.
A unit of expression of energy equal to 1000 calories is called a Kilocalorie. The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a kilogram or liter of water 1°Celsius.
Force is called Kinetic.
The combination and interrelation of the nervous, muscular, and skeletal systems is called the Kinetic Chain.
A method of feedback that provides information about the quality of the movement pattern performed is called Knowledge of Performance (KP).
A method of feedback after the completion of a movement to inform the client about the outcome of their performance is called Knowledge of Results (KR).
Exaggerated outward curvature of the thoracic region of the spinal column resulting in a rounded upper back is called Kyphosis.
An acid produced by glucose-burning cells when these cells have an insufficient supply of oxygen is called Lactic Acid.
Positioned toward the outside of the body is called Lateral.
The bending of the spine (cervical, thoracic, or lumbar) from side to side is called Lateral Flexion.

Acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the size of the force causing it, in the same direction as the force, and inversely proportional to the size of the object, according to the Law of Acceleration.
Every force produced by one object onto another produces an opposite force of equal magnitude, according to the Law of Action-Reaction.

Two bodies have an attraction to each other that is directly proportional to their masses and inversely proportional to the square of their distance from each other, according to the Law of Gravitation.
Weight reduction can only take place when there is more energy burned than consumed, according to the Law of Thermodynamics.
The resting length of a muscle and the tension the muscle can produce at this resting length is called the Length-Tension Relationship.
White blood cells are called Leukocytes.
Primary connective tissue that connects bones together and provides stability, input to the nervous system, guidance, and the limitation of improper joint movement is called Ligament.
The maximum force a muscle can produce in a single contraction is called Limit Strength.
A group of compounds that includes triglycerides (fats and oils), phospholipids, and sterols is called Lipids.
A characteristic of bone that has a long cylindric body with irregular or widened bony ends is called Long Bones. Examples include the clavicle and humerus.
A muscle fiber arrangement in which its fibers run parallel to the line of pull is called Longitudinal Muscle Fiber. An example would include the sartorius.
The lower brain includes the brainstem, basal ganglia, and cerebellum.
An individual with lower-extremity postural distortion has increased lumbar lordosis and an anterior pelvic tilt.
The lumbar spine, commonly referred to as the small of the back, is located between the thorax (chest) and the pelvis.
The lumbo-pelvic-hip complex involves the anatomic structures of the lumbar and thoracic spines, the pelvic girdle, and the hip joint.
Lumbo-pelvic-hip postural distortion is characterized by altered joint mechanics that lead to increased lumbar extension and decreased hip extension.
Maximal oxygen consumption (VO2max) is the highest rate of oxygen transport and utilization achieved at maximal physical exertion.
Maximal strength is the maximum force an individual’s muscle can produce in a single voluntary effort, regardless of the rate of force production.
Mechanical specificity refers to the specific muscular exercises using different weights and movements that are performed to increase strength or endurance in certain body parts.
Mechanical specificity also refers to the weight and movements placed on the body.
Mechanoreceptors are sensory receptors responsible for sensing distortion in body tissues.
Medial means positioned near the middle of the body.
The mediastinum is the space in the chest between the lungs that contains all the internal organs of the chest except the lungs.
The medullary cavity is the central cavity of bone shafts where marrow is stored.
Metabolic specificity refers to the specific muscular exercises using different levels of energy that are performed to increase endurance, strength, or power.
Metabolic specificity also refers to the energy demand placed on the body.
Metabolism encompasses all the chemical reactions that occur in the body to maintain itself.
Metabolism is the process in which nutrients are acquired, transported, used, and disposed of by the body.
Mitochondria are the principal energy source of the cell.
Mitochondria convert nutrients into energy as well as performing many other specialized tasks.
The M-line is the portion of the sarcomere where the myosin filaments connect with very thin filaments called titin and create an anchor for the structures of the sarcomere.
Mode refers to the type of exercise performed.
Momentum is the product of the size of the object (mass) and its velocity (speed with which it is moving).
A monthly plan is a generalized training plan that spans one month and shows which phases will be required each day of each week.

Motor behavior is the manner in which the nervous, skeletal, and muscular systems interact to produce an observable mechanical response to the incoming sensory information from the internal and external environments.
Motor behavior is also a motor response to internal and external environmental stimuli.
Motor control involves the structures and mechanisms that the nervous system uses to gather sensory information and integrate it with previous experiences to produce a motor response.
Motor control is also how the central nervous system integrates internal and external sensory information with previous experiences to produce a motor response.
Motor development is the change in motor skill behavior over time throughout the lifespan.
Motor (function of nervous system) is the neuromuscular response to the sensory information.
Motor learning is the integration of motor control processes with practice and experience that lead to relatively permanent changes in the capacity to produce skilled movements.
Motor (efferent) neurons transmit nerve impulses from the brain and spinal cord to effector sites.
A motor unit consists of a motor neuron and all of the muscle fibers it innervates.
Multipenniform muscles have multiple tendons with obliquely running muscle fibers.
The multiple-set system consists of performing multiple sets of the same exercise.
A multisensory condition is a training environment that provides heightened stimulation to proprioceptors and mechanoreceptors.
The muscle action spectrum includes concentric, eccentric, and isometric actions.
Muscle fiber arrangement refers to the manner in which the fibers are situated in relation to the tendon.
Muscle fiber recruitment refers to the recruitment pattern of muscle fibers or motor units in response to creating force for a specific movement.
Muscle hypertrophy is characterized by the increase in the cross-sectional area of individual muscle fibers and is believed to result from an increase in the myofibril proteins.
Muscle hypertrophy is also the enlargement of skeletal muscle fibers in response to overcoming force from high volumes of tension.
Muscle imbalance is an alteration of muscle length surrounding a joint.
Muscle spindles are receptors sensitive to change in the length of the muscle and the rate of that change.
Muscle synergies are groups of muscles that are recruited by the central nervous system to provide movement.
Muscular endurance is a muscle’s ability to contract for an extended period.
Muscular endurance is also the ability to produce and maintain force production over prolonged periods of time.
The muscular system is a series of muscles that moves the skeleton.
Myofibrils are a portion of muscle that contains myofilaments.
Myofilaments are the contractile components of muscle, actin, and myosin.
Myosin is one of the two major myofilaments known as the thick filament that works with actin to produce muscular contraction.
The nervous system is a conglomeration of billions of cells specifically designed to provide a communication network within the human body.
Neural Activation is the contraction of a muscle generated by neural stimulation.
Neural Adaptation is an adaptation to strength training in which muscles are under the direct command of the nervous system.
Neuromuscular Efficiency is the ability of the neuromuscular system to enable all muscles to efficiently work together in all planes of motion.

Neuromuscular Efficiency is the ability of the neuromuscular system to allow agonists, antagonists, and stabilizers to work synergistically to produce, reduce, and dynamically stabilize the entire kinetic chain in all three planes of motion.
The Neuromuscular Junction is the point at which the neuron meets the muscle to allow the action potential to continue its impulse.
Neuromuscular Specificity is the specific muscular exercises using different speeds and styles that are performed to increase neuromuscular efficiency.
Neuromuscular Specificity refers to the speed of contraction and exercise selection.
A Neuron is the functional unit of the nervous system.
Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers that cross the neuromuscular junction (synapse) to transmit electrical impulses from the nerve to the muscle.
A Neutralizer is a muscle that counteracts the unwanted action of other muscles.
Nociceptors are sensory receptors that respond to pain.
Nonsynovial Joints are joints that do not have a joint cavity, connective tissue, or cartilage.

The Novice Stage is the first stage of the dynamic pattern perspective model in which the learner simplifies movements by minimizing the specific timing of joint motions, which tends to result in movement that is rigid and jerky.
Nutrition is the process by which a living organism assimilates food and uses it for growth and repair of tissues.
Obesity is the condition of being considerably overweight, and refers to a person with a body mass index of 30 or greater, or who is at least 30 pounds over the recommended weight for their height.
Obesity is the condition of subcutaneous fat exceeding the amount of lean body mass.
The Occipital Lobe is a portion of the cerebral cortex that deals with vision.
Optimal Strength is the ideal level of strength that an individual needs to perform functional activities.

Optimum Performance Training™ (OPT™) is a systematic, integrated, and functional training program that simultaneously improves an individual’s biomotor abilities and builds high levels of functional strength, neuromuscular efficiency, and dynamic flexibility.
The Origin is the more fixed, central, or larger attachment of a muscle—compare with insertion.
Osteoarthritis is arthritis in which cartilage becomes soft, frayed, or thins out, as a result of trauma or other conditions.
Osteoblasts are a type of cell that is responsible for bone formation.
Osteoclasts are a type of bone cell that removes bone tissue.
Osteopenia is a decrease in the calcification or density of bone as well as reduced bone mass.
Osteoporosis is a condition in which there is a decrease in bone mass and density as well as an increase in the space between bones, resulting in porosity and fragility.
Overtraining is excessive frequency, volume, or intensity of training, resulting in fatigue, which is also caused by a lack of proper rest and recovery.
Overweight refers to a person with a body mass index of 25 to 29, or who is between 25 to 30 pounds over the recommended weight for their height.
Oxygen Uptake is the usage of oxygen by the body.
Oxygen Uptake Reserve (V. o 2 R) is the difference between resting and maximal or peak oxygen consumption.
The Parietal Lobe is a portion of the cerebral cortex that is involved with sensory information.
Pattern Overload is repetitive physical activity that moves through the same patterns of motion, placing the same stresses on the body over time.
Pattern Overload is consistently repeating the same pattern of motion, which may place abnormal stresses on the body.
Perception is the integrating of sensory information with past experiences or memories.
Perimysium is the connective tissue that surrounds fascicles.
Periodization is the division of a training program into smaller, progressive stages.

Periosteum is a dense membrane composed of fibrous connective tissue that closely wraps (invests) all bone, except that of the articulating surfaces in joints, which are covered by a synovial membrane.
Peripheral Arterial Disease is a condition characterized by narrowing of the major arteries that are responsible for supplying blood to the lower extremities.

Peripheral Heart Action System (PHA) is a variation of circuit training in which the client performs four to six exercises in a row, rests for 30 to 45 seconds, then moves to the next sequence of different exercise and continues the pattern.
Peripheral Nervous System is cranial and spinal nerves that spread throughout the body.
Peripheral Vascular Disease is a group of diseases in which blood vessels become restricted or blocked, typically as a result of atherosclerosis.
Phases of Training are smaller divisions of training progressions that fall within the three building blocks of training.
Photoreceptors are sensory receptors that respond to light (vision).
Physical Activity Readiness Questionnaire (PAR-Q) is a questionnaire that has been designed to help qualify a person for low-to-moderate-to-high activity levels.
Pivot Joint allows movement in predominately the transverse plane; examples would include the atlantoaxial joint at the base of the skull and between the radioulnar joint.
Plane of Motion refers to the plane (sagittal, frontal, or transverse) in which the exercise is performed.
Plantarflexion is ankle motion such that the toes are pointed toward the ground.
Plasma is an aqueous liquidlike component of blood.
Plyometric (Reactive) Training are exercises that generate quick, powerful movements involving an explosive concentric muscle contraction preceded by an eccentric muscle action.
Posterior (Dorsal) is on the back of the body.
Posterior Pelvic Tilt is a movement in which the pelvis rotates backward.
Postural Distortion Patterns are predictable patterns of muscle imbalances.
Postural Equilibrium is the ability to efficiently maintain balance throughout the body segments.
Posture is the position and bearing of the body for alignment and function of the kinetic chain.
Power is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest force in the shortest time.
Pregnancy is the condition of a female who contains an unborn child within the body.
Preprogrammed activation of muscles in healthy people occurs automatically and independently of other muscles before movement.
Prime Mover is the muscle that acts as the initial and main source of motive power.
The Principle of Individualism refers to the uniqueness of a program to the client for whom it is designed.

The Principle of Overload implies that there must be a training stimulus provided that exceeds the current capabilities of the kinetic chain to elicit the optimal physical, physiologic, and performance adaptations.
The Principle of Progression refers to the intentional manner in which a program is designed to progress according to the physiologic capabilities of the kinetic chain and the goals of the client.
The Principle of Specificity or Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demands (SAID Principle) states that the body will adapt to the specific demands that are placed on it.
Processes are projections protruding from the bone where muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach.
Program Design is a purposeful system or plan put together to help an individual achieve a specific goal.
Pronation is a triplanar movement that is associated with force reduction.
Proprioception is the cumulative sensory input to the central nervous system from all mechanoreceptors that sense body position and limb movements.

A Proprioceptively Enriched Environment is an unstable (yet controllable) physical situation in which exercises are performed that causes the body to use its internal balance and stabilization mechanisms.
Protein is amino acids linked by peptide bonds, which consist of carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, and usually sulfur, and that have several essential biologic compounds.
Proximal is positioned nearest the center of the body or point of reference.
Deoxygenated blood is pumped from the right ventricle to the lungs through Pulmonary Arteries.
Pulmonary Capillaries surround the alveolar sacs. As oxygen fills the sacs it diffuses across the capillary membranes and into the bloodstream.
The Pulmonary Semilunar Valve controls blood flow from the right ventricle to the pulmonary arteries going to the lungs.
The Pyramid System involves a triangle or step approach that either progresses up in weight with each set or decreases weight with each set.
Pyruvate is a byproduct of anaerobic glycolysis.
Quadrilateral muscle fiber is an arrangement of muscle fibers that is usually flat and four-sided. An example would include the rhomboid.
Quickness is the ability to react and change body position with maximal rate of force production, in all planes of motion and from all body positions, during functional activities.
Range of motion refers to the range that the body or bodily segments move during an exercise.
Rapport is an aspect of a relationship characterized by similarity, agreement, or congruity.
Muscular force can be amplified by increasing the rate of incoming impulses from the motor neuron after all prospective motor units have been activated.
Rate of force production is the ability of muscles to exert maximal force output in a minimal amount of time.
Reactive strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to switch from an eccentric contraction to a concentric contraction quickly and efficiently.
Reactive training are exercises that use quick, powerful movements involving an eccentric contraction immediately followed by an explosive concentric contraction.
Reciprocal inhibition is the simultaneous contraction of one muscle and the relaxation of its antagonist to allow movement to take place.

Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) is the average daily nutrient intake level that is sufficient to meet the nutrient requirement of nearly all (97 to 98%) healthy individuals who are in a particular life stage and gender group.
Recreation is a client’s physical activities outside of their work environment.
Relative flexibility is the tendency of the body to seek the path of least resistance during functional movement patterns.
Relative strength is the maximum force that an individual can generate per unit of body weight, regardless of the time of force development.
Remodeling is the process of resorption and formation of bone.
Repetition is one complete movement of a single exercise.
Repetition tempo is the speed with which each repetition is performed.
Resistance development is the second stage of the General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), when the body increases its functional capacity to adapt to the stressor.
Respiratory passageway collects the channelled air coming from the conducting passageway.

Respiratory pump is composed of skeletal structures (bones) and soft tissues (muscles) that work together to allow proper respiratory mechanics to occur and help pump blood back to the heart during inspiration.
Respiratory system is a system of organs (the lungs and respiratory passageways) that collects oxygen from the external environment and transports it to the bloodstream.
Rest interval is the time taken to recuperate between sets.
Restrictive lung disease is the condition of a fibrous lung tissue, which results in a decreased ability to expand the lungs.
Rheumatoid Arthritis is a type of arthritis that primarily affects connective tissues.
Rheumatoid Arthritis involves thickening of articular soft tissue and extension of synovial tissue over eroded articular cartilages.
Roll is a type of joint motion that depicts the rolling of one joint surface on another.
An example of roll is the femoral condyles rolling over the tibial condyles during a squat.
Root Cause Analysis is a method of asking questions step-by-step to discover the initial cause of a fault.
Rotary Motion is the movement of bones around joints.
A Saddle Joint is a type of joint where one bone is shaped as a saddle and the other bone is shaped as the rider.
The only example of a Saddle Joint is in the carpometacarpal joint in the thumb.
The Sagittal Plane is an imaginary bisector that divides the body into left and right halves.
The Sarcolemma is a plasma membrane that surrounds muscle fibers.
A Sarcomere is the functional unit of muscle that produces muscular contraction and consists of repeating sections of actin and myosin.
Sarcoplasm contains glycogen, fats, minerals, and oxygen within the sarcolemma.
Scapular Depression is the downward (inferior) motion of the scapula.
Scapular Elevation is the upward (superior) motion of the scapula.
Scapular Protraction is the abduction of the scapula; shoulder blades move away from the midline.
Scapular Retraction is the adduction of the scapula; shoulder blades move toward the midline.
Self-Myofascial Release is a form of flexibility that focuses on the fascial system in the body.
Self-Organization is a theory based on the dynamic pattern perspective that provides the body with the ability to overcome changes.
Semilunar Valves allow for proper blood flow from the ventricles to the aorta and pulmonary arteries.
Sensation is the process whereby sensory information is received by the receptor and transferred to the spinal cord for reflexive motor behavior or to higher cortical areas for processing.
Sensorimotor Integration is the ability of the nervous system to gather and interpret sensory information to anticipate, select, and execute the proper motor response.
Sensorimotor Integration is also the cooperation of the nervous and muscular system in gathering and interpreting information and executing movement.
Sensors provide feedback from the effectors to the central controller and cardiovascular control system.
Sensors include baroreceptors, chemoreceptors, and muscle afferents.
Sensory Feedback is the process whereby sensory information is used to reactively monitor movement and the environment.
Sensory (Function of Nervous System) is the ability of the nervous system to sense changes in either the internal or external environment.
A Set is a group of consecutive repetitions.
Sensory (Afferent) Neurons transmit nerve impulses from effector sites (such as muscles and organs) via receptors to the brain and spinal cord.
Short bones appear cubical in shape.
Examples of short bones include the carpals and tarsals.
The single-set system involves performing one set of each exercise, usually 8 to 12 repetitions at a slow, controlled tempo.
The sinoatrial (SA) node is a specialized area of cardiac tissue located in the right atrium of the heart.
The sinoatrial (SA) node initiates the electrical impulses that determine the heart rate and is often termed the pacemaker for the heart.
The skeletal system is the body’s framework composed of bones and joints.
A skin-fold caliper is an instrument with two adjustable legs used to measure the thickness of a skin fold.
Slide is a joint motion that depicts the sliding of one joint surface across another.
An example of slide joint motion is the tibial condyles moving across the femoral condyles during a knee extension.
The sliding filament theory is the proposed process by which the contraction of the filaments within the sarcomere takes place.
Slow twitch fibers are another term for type I muscle fibers.
Slow twitch fibers are characterized by a greater amount of capillaries, mitochondria, and myoglobin.
Slow twitch fibers usually have a higher endurance capacity than fast twitch fibers.
A specific warm-up consists of low-intensity exercise consisting of movements that mimic those that will be included in the more intense exercise that is to follow.
Speed is the ability to move the body in one intended direction as fast as possible.
Speed strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest possible force in the shortest possible time.
Spin is a joint motion that depicts the rotation of one joint surface on another.
An example of spin joint motion is the head of the radius rotating on the end of the humerus during pronation and supination of the forearm.
The split-routine system incorporates training an individual’s body parts with a high volume on separate days.
Stability is the ability of the body to maintain postural equilibrium and support joints during movement.
Stabilization endurance is the ability of the stabilization mechanisms of the kinetic chain to sustain proper levels of stabilization to allow for prolonged neuromuscular efficiency.
Stabilization strength is the ability of the stabilizing muscles to provide dynamic joint stabilization and postural equilibrium during functional activities.
Stabilizers are muscles that support or stabilize the body while the prime movers and synergists perform the movement patterns.
Starting strength is the ability to produce high levels of force at the beginning of a movement.
Static stretching is the process of passively taking a muscle to the point of tension and holding the stretch for a minimum of 30 seconds.
Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension to overcome an external load.
Strength endurance is the ability of the body to repeatedly produce high levels of force for prolonged periods.
Stride length is the distance covered with each stride.
Stride rate is the number of strides taken in a given amount of time (or distance).
Stroke Volume (SV) is the amount of blood pumped out of the heart with each contraction.
Structural Efficiency is the structural alignment of the muscular and skeletal systems that allows the body to be balanced in relation to its center of gravity.
Subjective information is provided by a client.
Substrates are the material or substance on which an enzyme acts.
Sucrose, often referred to as table sugar, is a molecule made up of glucose and fructose.
A sulcus is a groove in a bone that allows a soft structure to pass through.
Superior means positioned above a point of reference.
A superset is a set of two exercises that are performed back-to-back, without any rest time between them.
Supination is a triplanar motion that is associated with force production.
Supine means lying on one’s back.
A synarthrosis joint is a joint without any joint cavity and fibrous connective tissue. Examples include the sutures of the skull and the symphysis pubis.
Synergist muscles assist prime movers during functional movement patterns.
Synergistic Dominance is when synergists take over function for a weak or inhibited prime mover.
Synovial joints are joints that are held together by a joint capsule and ligaments and are most associated with movement in the body.
The temporal lobe is a portion of the cerebral cortex that deals with hearing.
Tendons are connective tissues that attach muscle to bone and provide an anchor for muscles to produce force.
Tendonitis is an inflammation in a tendon or the tendon covering.
The thoracic spine is the 12 vertebrae in the mid-torso that are attached to the rib cage.
Time is the length of time an individual is engaged in a given activity.

The Tolerable Upper Intake Level (UL) is the highest average daily nutrient intake level likely to pose no risk of adverse health effects to almost all individuals in a particular life stage and gender group. As intake increases above the UL, the potential risk of adverse health effects increases.
Torque is the ability of any force to cause rotation around an axis.
Training Duration is the timeframe of a workout or the length of time spent in one phase of training.
Training Frequency is the number of training sessions performed during a specified period (usually 1 week).
Training Intensity is an individual’s level of effort, compared with their maximal effort, which is usually expressed as a percentage.

A Training Plan is the specific outline, created by a fitness professional to meet a client’s goals, that details the form of training, length of time, future changes, and specific exercises to be performed.
Training Volume is the amount of physical training performed within a specified period.
The Transfer-of-Training Effect is that the more similar the exercise is to the actual activity, the greater the carryover into real-life settings.
The Transverse Plane is an imaginary bisector that divides the body into top and bottom halves.
The Tricuspid Valve controls the blood flow from the right atrium to the right ventricle.
Triglycerides are the chemical or substrate form in which most fat exists in food as well as in the body.
A Tri-Sets System is a system very similar to supersets, the difference being three exercises back to back to back with little to no rest in between.
A trochanter is a projection protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach; also known as a condyle, process, tubercle, and epicondyle.
A tubercle is a projection protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach; also known as a condyle, process, epicondyle, and trochanter.
Type refers to the type or mode of physical activity that an individual is engaged in.
Unipenniform muscle fibers are muscle fibers that are arranged with short, oblique fibers that extend from one side of a long tendon. An example would include the tibialis posterior.
Upper-Extremity Postural Distortion is an individual who exhibits a forward head, rounded shoulder posture.

The Valsalva Maneuver is a maneuver in which a person tries to exhale forcibly with a closed glottis (windpipe) so that no air exits through the mouth or nose as, for example, in lifting a heavyweight. The Valsalva maneuver impedes the return of venous blood to the heart.
Veins are vessels that transport blood from the capillaries toward the heart.
Ventilation is the actual process of moving air in and out of the body.

Ventilatory Threshold is the point during graded exercise in which ventilation increases disproportionately to oxygen uptake, signifying a switch from predominantly aerobic energy production to anaerobic energy production.
Ventral refers to a position on the front or toward the front of the body.
Ventricles are the inferior chamber of the heart that receives blood from its corresponding atrium and, in turn, forces blood into the arteries.
Venules are the very small veins that connect capillaries to the larger veins.
The Vertebral Column is a series of irregularly shaped bones called vertebrae that houses the spinal cord.
Vertical Loading is alternating body parts trained from set to set, starting from the upper extremity and moving to the lower extremity.
VO2max is the highest rate of oxygen transport and utilization achieved at maximal physical exertion.
A Weekly Plan is a training plan of specific workouts that spans 1 week and shows which exercises are required each day of the week.
